description:
[ARCHIVED 2026-05-08 — obsolete under MIL-AXO-015 / REQ-AXO-200 PG migration. Per-batch Parquet small-files explosion is moot once the Parquet store is decommissioned (REQ-AXO-200 deliverable). pgvector HNSW handles arbitrary insert volume natively without partition fan-out. Original brief preserved below.]

## Original scope

DEC-AXO-073 L.1 + DEC-AXO-074 M.1.1 adopted per-batch Parquet file design (each `append_batch` writes a self-contained file). Unblocked mid-stream readability but created N-files-grow-with-time scaling. Required a background compaction job. Under PG/pgvector, no compaction concept needed — the HNSW index is mutated in-place.

## Tags

performance, parquet, compaction, dec-axo-073-followup, archived-by-pg-migration

SOLL = canonical intentional truth layer of every Axon-equipped project, NOT just Axon itself. Survives sessions, humans, releases. IST captures what code IS; SOLL captures what code SHOULD BE: visions, requirements, decisions, concepts, milestones, validations, stakeholders, guidelines.

## Surface
- Read: soll_query_context, soll_work_plan (returns topological order of unblockers, scored by descendant impact — authoritative for 'what to work on next', ahead of personal judgment).
- Write: soll_manager / infer_soll_mutation / entrench_nuance / soll_apply_plan only. Cypher INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE forbidden (read-only by spec). soll_manager(create entity=guideline) supported as of REQ-AXO-092.

## Nine canonical entity types
| Type | Role |
|---|---|
| Vision | North Star; human+business value; ~1-2x/year change |
| Pillar | Strategic axis epitomizing Vision (this = PIL-AXO-003) |
| Requirement | Actionable work: bugs, simplifications, robustness gaps, customer-deliverability findings |
| Concept | Shared mental model grouping related entities |
| Decision | Chosen approach + rationale (immutable history) |
| Milestone | Time-anchored delivery target |
| Validation | Proof-of-intent (evidence) |
| Stakeholder | Human or org with stake in outcome |
| Guideline | Working method, applied at delivery time by axon_pre_flight_check |

## Operational protocol (any LLM session, any project with Axon MCP access; standing rule set 2026-05-01 by Didier; enforced via /home/dstadel/.claude/CLAUDE.md)
1. Observe friction, bugs, simplifications, obsolete elements, LLM-contract violations.
2. Log to SOLL via soll_manager. Even partial-analysis findings are valuable.
3. Trace dependencies by linking to a Concept or Pillar.
4. Re-evaluate by calling soll_work_plan after each batch.
5. Execute relentlessly through wave-1 unblockers. Interrupt user only for: destructive irreversible / arch decision needing human authority / hard blocker / external-impact milestone (NOT internal velocity).

## Rationale (terse)
Without this Pillar enforced, Axon = passive structural code index, missing half the promise. Customer value = any team adopting Axon inherits a queryable, self-correcting institutional memory that scales with the team and outlives any one contributor.

ONE telemetry-backed snapshot of runtime + IST + project scope, observable identically by every consumer (humans/dashboards, LLMs/MCP, automation/CLI).

## Operational rules
- status() = canonical readiness call, NOT polite probe. HEALTHY MUST be backed by liveness evidence: pid alive, role-port bound BY THAT pid, telemetry socket recently emitted, guard owner = running pid (REQ-AXO-097 = inverse failure: HEALTHY despite dead role processes).
- mcp_surface_diagnostics offers deeper truth when status looks suspicious. MUST agree with status when system is healthy.
- **Data ownership convention (post MIL-AXO-015 PG)**: brain and indexer share the same PG instance; under PG MVCC both read AND write concurrently. They are conventionally separated by which tables they own as authoritative writer:
  - Indexer owns the IST: `public.file` (or its successor `IndexedFile` post REQ-AXO-289), `public.symbol`, `public.chunk`, `public.chunkembedding`, AGE edges. Writes the graph + vectors as source of truth.
  - Brain owns intent + audit: `soll.Node`, `soll.Edge`, audit tables (`McpJob`, `axon_runtime.OptimizerDecisionLog`). Reads IST/vectors for retrieval, writes SOLL for mutations.
  - PG enforces no lock between them — the separation is by-convention, not by-mechanism. Any code, comment, or doc framing brain as "read-only" or asserting a single-writer constraint is DuckDB-era residue (cf. CPT-AXO-053) and must be corrected on sight.
- project_code: auto-resolved from cwd or AXON_PROJECT_ROOT, deterministic across surfaces (REQ-AXO-089 = retrieve_context not honoring resolution).

## Acceptance
- status() and project_status() never disagree on HEALTHY/DEGRADED/DOWN within same window.
- Dead role process → status transitions to DEGRADED within one polling interval. Never stuck HEALTHY.
- `axon-live status` (CLI) and `mcp__axon__status` (MCP) emit identical evidence fields.
- Broken state → status surface tells you exactly what+where, never "maybe it works".

## Rationale (terse)
Value of Axon = single trusted source of structural+operational truth. Multi-truth Axon < no Axon (it pretends).

## Recent corrections (2026-05-12)
- DuckDB-era "brain = IST READER" framing removed (operator critique session 17): inaccurate under PG MVCC where both products are independent multi-writers. Replaced with the data-ownership-by-convention model above. The strict reader/writer split was a DuckDB single-writer mutex artefact — see CPT-AXO-030 (superseded) for archaeology.

## STATUS — SUPERSEDED 2026-05-12 (session 17) by REQ-AXO-289 streaming pipeline v2

The orphan reaper described here patches the symptom (files stuck in `status='indexing'` after indexer restarts). REQ-AXO-289 eliminates the symptom STRUCTURALLY: there is no `public.file` status machine in v2, therefore no claim state to orphan, therefore no orphans to reap. The reaper becomes dead code.

**The local commit `4837bd7` that implemented this REQ will be reverted** in session 17 closure — we don't ship dette-perpétuante code even when it works. Cleaner to start from a baseline that matches v2's design intent.

**Canonical successor**: REQ-AXO-289 (no orphans by design — idempotent UPSERT + no claim state in DB).

## Historical content (session 17 investigation + reaper implementation, reverted)

## Symptom

Live `axon_live.public.file` snapshot 2026-05-12 session 17:

| status   | count  |
|---|---|
| indexing | 12,189 |
| indexed  |  1,889 |
| skipped  |     72 |
| **total** | **14,150** |

86% of files stuck in `indexing`. Claimed by `claim_pending_paths` then orphaned by indexer crashes; no startup reaper.

## What was implemented (commit `4837bd7`, to be reverted)

- `GraphStore::reset_orphaned_indexing_rows(stale_after_ms)` in `graph_ingestion.rs`.
- Wiring in `runtime_boot.rs` at indexer startup, inside `if runtime_mode.ingestion_enabled()`.
- Unit test with 3 fixtures (stale / NULL / fresh) + idempotency + fresh-DB safety.
- All 1165 lib tests pass.

## Why reverted

Operator session 17 critique (verbatim): the patch perpetuates the DuckDB-era state machine. The right answer is to remove the state machine. Patches on top of dette = more code to maintain, more places where the model can mislead future LLMs.

## Cross-references

- REQ-AXO-289 (canonical successor) — v2 eliminates orphans by design.
- REQ-AXO-285 (also superseded) — pipeline A symptoms, same root cause.
- Commit `4837bd7` (local, never pushed) — the implementation, to be reverted.

## Tags

`functionality_key:throughput-north-star`, `pipeline-a`, `data-hygiene`, **`superseded-by-req-axo-289`**, **`commit-4837bd7-reverted`**, **`patch-of-dette-not-shipped`**

## Originator

Claude session 17 — 2026-05-12 investigation of REQ-AXO-285 symptom 2 surfaced the orphan accumulation pattern. Implementation revealed it as patch-of-dette; structural answer = REQ-AXO-289.

## Umbrella REQ for streaming pipeline v2

Direct implementation of the architecture canonicalized in CPT-AXO-053 + CPT-AXO-054. Decided session 17 (2026-05-12) after operator critique of accumulated over-engineering. Approach: replace the legacy DuckDB-era multi-layer ingestion machine (CPT-AXO-026's 16-layer audit) with a 6-stage streaming pipeline using bounded `tokio::sync::mpsc` channels, per-stage worker pools, idempotent PG UPSERT, and zero machine-à-états in the DB.

## Scope — what this REQ ships

### Code (new)
- `src/axon-core/src/pipeline_v2/` module: StageMetrics, worker pool helper, channel topology, A1…B3 stage implementations.
- `IndexedFile(path PK, content_hash, last_seen_ms)` table DDL + arc-swap<DashMap> in-RAM filter.
- Feature flag `AXON_PIPELINE_V2=1` for coexistence during validation.
- Per-stage metrics surface (in-memory) + brain `status` MCP tool integration + dashboard panel.
- Cold-start poll for B1: `SELECT chunk_id FROM Chunk LEFT JOIN ChunkEmbedding ce ON ce.chunk_id=Chunk.id WHERE ce.chunk_id IS NULL LIMIT N`.
- Configurable per-stage worker counts via env vars (`AXON_A1_WORKERS` … `AXON_B3_WORKERS`).
- Bench scripts for v2 (files/s + chunks/s).

### Code (deleted at slice S7+S8 cut-over)
- `spawn_autonomous_ingestor`, `claim_policy`, `plan_admissions`, `fill_admission_plan`, `claim_pending_paths`, `requeue_claimed_paths_with_reason`, `mark_pending_files_deferred`, `mark_file_oversized_for_current_budget` and all callers.
- `spawn_ingress_promoter`, `promote_ingress_batch`, `SharedIngressBuffer`, `bootstrap_salvage_paths`, `should_suppress_bootstrap_event_storm`.
- `spawn_writer_actor`, `DbWriteTask`, `writer_ctx` mutex in `graph.rs:33` (REQ-AXO-283 scope).
- `public.file` table (all 23 columns), `FileVectorizationQueue` (subsumed by B's channel + poll DB).
- `claim_mode`, `admission_target_band`, `exhaustion_ratio`, `oversized_for_current_budget`, `defer_count`, all related gates.
- The orphan reaper `reset_orphaned_indexing_rows` (REQ-AXO-288, no orphans to reap under v2).
- Reaper sidecar `writer-actor.trace`, `vector-lane.trace` if they reference DuckDB-era only state.

### Documentation
- CPT-AXO-053 + CPT-AXO-054 (DONE this session).
- CLAUDE.md (project) point to CPT-AXO-053 as canonical product split.
- Skill axon-engineering-protocol updated with v2 vocabulary.
- Two env config templates (cf. REQ-AXO-290 child).

## Acceptance criteria

AC1. `cargo test --lib --bins` green across all stage workers + idempotence tests.
AC2. Bench `./scripts/axon-dev bench-v2 --duration 5m` shows: files/s and chunks/s metrics per pipeline; backpressure_blocks per stage; identification of dominant bottleneck stage.
AC3. v2 throughput on dev DB ≥ legacy throughput on the same hardware for both files/s and chunks/s (`axon_pipeline_a_files_per_sec` and `axon_pipeline_b_chunks_per_sec`). Empirical baseline = sessions 14-16 figures (47.84 ch/s peak under legacy).
AC4. Crash at any stage → restart → watcher redetects diffs → no duplicate Symbol/Chunk/ChunkEmbedding rows (verified by checksum on a 1000-file repro).
AC5. Brain restart does not lose pipeline state; indexer restart does not produce orphan rows in any persisted table (the only persisted state is the graph itself + IndexedFile filter).
AC6. Activating `AXON_RUNTIME_MODE=indexer_graph` alone keeps Pipeline B silent (no GPU workers, no B-stage threads). Activating `AXON_RUNTIME_MODE=indexer_vector` alone runs B against existing graph state.
AC7. After cut-over (slice S7), `public.file` table no longer exists in schema; migration script handles drop cleanly.
AC8. Env config templates ship in repo (REQ-AXO-290).
AC9. `soll_validate` zero violations after this REQ lands.

## Slices (already detailed in CPT-AXO-054 implementation section)

S1. Pipeline-v2 crate scaffolding + StageMetrics.
S2. IndexedFile table + arc-swap cache + watcher integration.
S3. A1→A2→A3 wired + equivalence vs legacy on dev DB.
S4. B1→B2→B3 wired (with cold-start poll).
S5. Per-stage metrics in `status` + dashboard panel.
S6. Bench v2 vs legacy.
S7. Flip default + delete legacy paths.
S8. Drop `public.file` table.
S9. REQ-AXO-290 env config templates.
S10. Doc + skills + session pointer update.

## Branch

`feat/pipeline-v2-streaming` (worktree-based or simple branch — dev work).

## Why P1

LLM operator critique session 17 (2026-05-12) verbatim:
> "On est en train d'inventer la roue avec nos systèmes d'indexation et on complexifie tellement... à la fin, ça devient tellement complexe qu'il n'y a plus rien qui fonctionne et toi tu n'y comprends rien et moi non plus."

Empirical evidence supporting urgency:
- Live DB has 12,189 files stuck in `status='indexing'` (86% of 14,150 total) — orphans accumulated across many indexer restarts.
- REQ-AXO-285 (pipeline A broken) is one of three symptoms (REQ-AXO-285/286/288/283) all rooted in the same DuckDB-era multi-layer-state-machine.
- Throughput north-star REQ-AXO-252 AC7 ≥1 50 ch/s gap = ~100 ch/s, primarily because pipeline A and B are coupled in throughput by state-machine contention (DuckDB-era artefact).
- Build complexity prevents productive operator + LLM session work — multi-hour debug just to understand the gate chain.

v2 fixes ALL of these simultaneously by structural simplification.

## Children REQs

- REQ-AXO-290 — environment config templates + obsolete param cleanup (filed concurrently).
- (more REQ children may be filed per-slice as needed; this umbrella scope-anchors them).

## Supersedes

- REQ-AXO-285 (pipeline A broken) — problem eliminated structurally.
- REQ-AXO-286 bug 2 (indexer rise gate) — no rise gate needed; v2 stages are uniform.
- REQ-AXO-288 (orphan reaper) — no orphans to reap.
- REQ-AXO-283 (LatticePool writer_ctx Mutex) — mutex eliminated.
- REQ-AXO-272 (vector worker topology) — generalized to per-stage worker pools.
- Multiple symptoms covered by CPT-AXO-026 16-layer audit — collapsed to 6 stages.

COMPLETED 2026-05-02. embedder.rs decomposition. Originally 10,313 LOC monolith with 6 worker loops + orchestration + helpers in single impl block.

=== Final state ===
emb edder.rs: 10,313 → 8,529 LOC (-1,784, -17.3%). All 6 worker loops extracted to dedicated submodules following the proven `use super::*` pattern.

| Phase | Commit | Worker loop | LOC saved |
|-------|--------|-------------|-----------|
| 1 | 2670b2b | vector_refill_worker_loop | 293 |
| 2 | 7dd2053 | vector_maintenance_worker_loop | 84 |
| 3 | 2fa7c36 | vector_prepare_worker_loop | 121 |
| 4 | e859832 | vector_persist_worker_loop | 96 |
| 5 | 23ccaaf | vector_finalize_worker_loop + process_vector_persist_outbox | 82 |
| 6 | 7a5913e | vector_worker_loop (1018 LOC hot path) + build_vector_embedding_model (93 LOC) | 1108 |

All commits: pure structural extraction (function body verbatim, behaviour-preserving), single caller spawn-site update, inline #[cfg(test)] linkage assertion (TDD gate REQ-AXO-121), zero new warnings, zero test regression.

=== Pattern reproductible ===
For a worker loop with no Self:: usage in body:
1. Create `embedder/<loop_name>.rs` with `use super::*;` + `pub(super) fn <loop>(...)` body verbatim.
2. Add `#[path = "..."] mod <loop_name>;` declaration in embedder.rs.
3. Update spawn site `Self::<loop>(...)` → `<loop_name>::<loop>(...)`.
4. Delete the function from impl block via sed.
5. Inline #[cfg(test)] linkage assertion in new file (TDD gate).
6. cargo build && cargo test --lib (suite stays ≥ baseline).

For a loop with one Self:: helper (vector_worker_loop case): co-locate the helper in the same new submodule. The Self::helper(...) call inside the loop becomes bare helper(...) since both are sibling free functions.

For cross-submodule helper calls: `use super::<sibling>::<helper>` reaches pub(super) items in sibling submodules.

=== Verification cumulative ===
- Test suite: 920 → 935 (+15: 6 new linkage assertions + 9 pre-existing tests now run reachable).
- Zero warning maintained throughout 6 commits.
- Production runtime not exercised in this session (no live promotion); pure structural refactor with byte-for-byte function bodies recommends low risk, but vector benchmark p50/p95 verification is recommended at next promotion to confirm no compiler-optimization-boundary impact on hot path.

stop.sh contains zero process-kill logic - pure delegation to axonctl. CONFIRMED COMPLETED 2026-05-02 hygiene pass: REQ-AXO-093 / REQ-AXO-101 / REQ-AXO-116 already delivered the bash-side cleanup; status was 'met' from a prior session, just flipped to 'completed' for index hygiene.
axonctl stop must handle Text file busy gracefully. CONFIRMED COMPLETED 2026-05-02 hygiene pass: status was 'met' from a prior session, just flipped to 'completed' for index hygiene.

**Problem**: the indexer pays the GPU cost to vectorize every chunk and persist `Chunk.embedding` rows in IST. Under the brain_only profile (the live MCP authority that serves all read-side LLM clients), the query-time embedding path is currently fail-fast (REQ-AXO-087): when an LLM calls `query`, `batch_embed` returns immediately with `Semantic fallback: embedding worker not in current profile`, the SQL engine never gets a query embedding, and the existing DuckDB `array_cosine_distance(s.embedding, $query_emb::FLOAT[DIMENSION])` path collapses to its structural-only branch. The persisted embeddings are dead weight from the brain's perspective; the brain literally does not deserve its name without semantic retrieval over its own canonical vectors. This was raised by the user (Didier) on 2026-05-02 in the context of REQ-AXO-124 part 2 (multi-token slow timeout).

**Required**: load an in-process ORT CPU embedding session in the brain at boot (or lazily on first query). Use the same ONNX model the indexer loads for chunk vectorization so the vector space is identical. Wire `batch_embed` to dispatch to the CPU session under brain_only and to the existing GPU subprocess under indexer_*. See DEC-AXO-061 for the chosen approach and rejected alternatives, CPT-AXO-022 for the architectural pattern.

**Acceptance criteria**:
1. **Functional**: under brain_only, `query("axonctl status")` (multi-token, currently times out per REQ-AXO-124 part 2) returns ranked results with `match_reason` indicating a semantic distance score, within 500 ms p95 on a graph of 50k Chunks. The persisted `Chunk.embedding` rows are scored against the query embedding via `array_cosine_distance`.
2. **Vector coherence**: the CPU session loads the same model artifact path (`runtime_embedding_snapshot_dir()/onnx/model.onnx`) and tokenizer that `OrtGpuFirstTextEmbedding::try_new(lane, idx, use_cuda=false)` uses today (gpu_backend.rs:130). Output dim matches `embedding_contract::DIMENSION`. A unit test asserts that the same input text produces the same vector (within float tolerance) whether embedded via the CPU in-process path or the GPU subprocess path.
3. **Lifecycle**: the embedder is created at brain boot (or on first call — implementation choice tracked in DEC-AXO-061). It is dropped cleanly on brain shutdown. It does not interfere with the GPU subprocess when both could exist (indexer-collocated brain).
4. **Routing**: `batch_embed` chooses the CPU in-process path when `current_query_embedding_sender()` returns `None` AND the runtime mode is brain_only (or any non-indexer profile that does not own a GPU subprocess). The fail-fast wording from REQ-AXO-087 ("embedding worker not in current profile") is replaced or no longer reachable under brain_only — update SKILL.md and REQ-AXO-087 to reflect.
5. **MCP contract**: the response surface in `axon_query` correctly labels the mode as `hybrid (structure + semantic similarity)` (already does so when `score` is in the SQL — just needs the embedding to be present). The `data.semantic_fallback_reason` field is `None` under brain_only when the CPU session is healthy.
6. **Tests**: (a) unit test for the CPU embedder returning a vector of the right dimension; (b) integration test that exercises `query` under brain_only with a fixture graph containing chunks with embeddings, asserting semantic ranking; (c) regression test that the indexer profile still uses the GPU subprocess.
7. **Documentation**: SKILL.md documents the brain_only semantic capability. REQ-AXO-087 is updated to describe the residual case (transient subprocess unavailability during indexer startup); the "profile-excluded" wording is retired.

**Out of scope** (tracked as future work):
- Activating DuckDB's VSS extension for sub-linear k-NN. Useful once chunk count reaches 100k+; until then, full-scan `array_cosine_distance` is acceptable.
- Reusing the CPU session for indexer ingestion (the indexer should keep using the GPU subprocess for throughput; the CPU path is single-query-oriented).

For `action=update` : - `data.status` remains optional (preserves existing value when absent). - If specified : same validation as create. ### Error envelope (calqué sur REQ-AXO-147 slice pattern) Reject hors-vocabulaire BEFORE the DB call returns : ```json { "isError": true, "data": { "status": "input_invalid", "parameter_repair": { "tool": "soll_manager", "category": "status", "invalid_field": "data.status", "supplied_value": "", "accepted_values": ["current", "planned", "delivered", "superseded", "rejected"], "normalization_hint": "", "canonical_source": "DEC-PRO-100", "follow_up_tools": ["soll_manager", "soll_query_context"], "hint": "retry with one of accepted_values ; default 'current' if you create a new actively-owned node" }, "example_valid_call": { "action": "create", "entity": "requirement", "data": { "project_code": "

", "title": "", "description": "", "status": "current" } } } } ``` ### Valid command examples (returned in parameter_repair on error) The server SHOULD include 1-3 `example_valid_call` shapes covering the most common patterns : 1. **Create with explicit status** (recommended) : ```json { "action": "create", "entity": "requirement", "data": { "project_code": "

", "title": "", "description": "", "status": "current" } } ``` 2. **Create with default fallback** (status omitted, server auto-fills 'current') : ```json { "action": "create", "entity": "requirement", "data": { "project_code": "

", "title": "", "description": "" } } ``` Server returns `data.notices: ["status auto-filled to 'current' per DEC-PRO-100 default"]`. 3. **Update status to terminal** : ```json { "action": "update", "entity": "requirement", "data": { "id": "REQ-XXX-NNN", "project_code": "

", "status": "delivered" } } ``` ### Normalization hint logic (server picks best suggestion) Map the supplied value via DEC-PRO-100 : - `completed`, `done`, `passed`, `closed`, `archived` → hint `delivered` (or `superseded` if outgoing SUPERSEDES edge exists at the target id) - `accepted`, `in_progress`, `active`, `open`, `proposed`, `partial`, `pending` → hint `current` (or `planned` if no activity evidence) - `failed` → hint `rejected` - anything else (typo, hallucination) → hint top-1 closest via Levenshtein on accepted_values Follow the existing pattern from `soll_manager` category=`entity` (unknown type → accepted_values list) and category=`relation_type` (forbidden link → `soll_relation_schema`). ## Implementation pointers - `src/axon-core/src/mcp/tools_soll/manager.rs:333` — the UPSERT path. ## Context
2026-05-14 curate-soll execution discovered that several intuitive supersession edges between same-type nodes are blocked by the canonical relation policy :

- `PIL -> PIL` SUPERSEDES → forbidden ("no canonical relation policy exists for this pair")
- `GUI -> GUI` SUPERSEDES → forbidden (allowed: INHERITS_FROM only)
- `REQ -> REQ` SUPERSEDES → forbidden (allowed: REFINES, BELONGS_TO)
- `CPT -> CPT` SUPERSEDES → forbidden (allowed: INHERITS_FROM, REFINES)

Workaround attempted : metadata `superseded_by` on the deprecated node + status='superseded'. Lossy : the graph structure doesn't carry the supersession ; only the metadata + status combination signals it.

## Real-world cases blocked
- `PIL-AXO-006 -> PIL-AXO-004` (Cohabitation live↔dev fusioned into PIL-004) : edge could not be created, only status update worked
- `GUI-AXO-1001 -> GUI-PRO-100` (transitional AXO guideline replaced by canonical PRO guideline post-registry-fix) : same
- `REQ-AXO-207/208 -> REQ-AXO-198` (P2 DDL generator duplicates) : same
- `CPT-AXO-038 -> CPT-AXO-037` (Storage Responsibility Shift duplicate) : same

## Why this matters
`soll_validate` already reports "Decisions without SOLVES/IMPACTS link" for orphan DEC. Same-type SUPERSEDES gap means : `soll_validate` can't enforce "node with status='superseded' MUST have outgoing SUPERSEDES edge" because the edge policy refuses to create one for the most common same-type cases.

## Required
Extend canonical relation policy in `src/axon-core/src/mcp/tools_soll/` to allow SUPERSEDES on these pairs :
- `PIL -> PIL` SUPERSEDES (when one pillar absorbs another)
- `GUI -> GUI` SUPERSEDES (when one guideline replaces another)
- `REQ -> REQ` SUPERSEDES (already partially blocked but should work for duplicates)
- `CPT -> CPT` SUPERSEDES (already partially blocked)

Reference location : `soll_relation_schema` MCP tool output + the policy table in `tools_soll/shared.rs` or equivalent.

## Acceptance
1. `soll_manager.link {source_id:'PIL-AXO-006', target_id:'PIL-AXO-004', relation_type:'SUPERSEDES'}` succeeds.
2. Same for GUI/GUI, REQ/REQ, CPT/CPT.
3. `soll_validate` can require SUPERSEDES edge on `status='superseded'` nodes.
4. Existing INHERITS_FROM / REFINES policies preserved for backward compat.

## Bridge
Related to REQ-AXO-323 (data-loss UPSERT) and REQ-AXO-325 (status validation). All 3 are `soll_manager` hardening REQs.

Shared mental model for the family of defects that prevent Axon from being delivered to customers AS IS because the runtime, MCP surface, or operations scripts present false-positive readiness, ambiguous LLM-facing messages, or silent-skip control flows. Discovered systematically during the Claude Sonnet 4.7 session on 2026-05-01. Members of this family - REQ-AXO-087 (embedding worker not ready ambiguity), REQ-AXO-088 (query empty answer with broken guidance promise), REQ-AXO-089 (retrieve_context auto-resolution does not honor cwd when project arg omitted), REQ-AXO-091 (soll_manager treats payload question marks as DuckDB placeholders), REQ-AXO-092 (soll_manager rejects guideline despite enum), REQ-AXO-093 (silent boot skip when orphan telemetry socket exists - ROOT CAUSE), REQ-AXO-094 (boot output mixes warnings system alarms info), REQ-AXO-095 (MCP tunnel verification failed but axon is rising), REQ-AXO-096 (dual toolchain mise plus devenv), REQ-AXO-097 (status returns HEALTHY when role processes are dead), REQ-AXO-098 (no degraded readiness contract), REQ-AXO-099 (24 plus tests fail in full suite), REQ-AXO-100 (operations doc says --full but flag is --indexer-full), REQ-AXO-101 (stop scripts leak telemetry sockets - ROOT CAUSE companion to 093), REQ-AXO-102 (start-brain not surfaced via unified entrypoint - partial), REQ-AXO-103 (SOLL_EXPORT files accumulate in docs/vision - partial), REQ-AXO-104 (status verbose tools list every call - partial), REQ-AXO-106 (status field Advanced indexed surfaces visible is opaque - partial), REQ-AXO-107 (cockpit floods log with SQL econnrefused). Core principle - any runtime or MCP output that an LLM might consume must be machine-actionable. State words like 'not ready' or 'failed' must distinguish design-exclusion from transient unavailability. Readiness signals must be backed by liveness probes, not just file existence. Stop scripts must clean up the artifacts they create. Operations doc and script flags must agree. The user (Didier) made this principle explicit on 2026-05-01 - if an LLM misunderstands an output, the output is wrong, not the LLM. Originator: Claude Sonnet 4.7 session 2026-05-01.

axon_pre_flight_check / axon_commit_work enforce GUI-PRO-001 by requiring a diff_path that matches the `tests.rs` pattern (substring `tests.rs`, suffix `_test.rs` / `_tests.rs` / `/tests.rs`, or path containing `/tests/`). The current matcher (src/axon-core/src/mcp/tools_soll/workflow_project.rs:3 `path_satisfies_required_path`) creates two friction surfaces:

(1) **Rust binary crates**: standard idiom `#[cfg(test)] mod tests { ... }` inline in the same file is rejected, forcing authors to split into a sibling `_tests.rs` file (see commit c6a1383 for axonctl).

(2) **Trivial library hygiene fixes**: any change to a `.rs` library file (e.g., adding `#[allow(dead_code)]`, fixing a comment, adjusting an attribute) is blocked unless a test file is co-modified — even when there is nothing meaningful to test for the cosmetic change itself, and the file already has comprehensive inline tests under `#[cfg(test)] mod tests {}`. Fix options: (a) extend `path_satisfies_required_path` to accept files that contain `#[cfg(test)]` markers when no co-modified test file exists, or (b) introduce a `inline_tests_allowed` metadata flag on the Guideline so strict-check scopes to logic-only changes, or (c) detect cosmetic-only diffs (only adds attributes / changes comments / whitespace) and skip the test-coverage gate, or (d) leave as-is and document the convention as 'always split binary tests to a sibling file' AND 'always co-modify a test even for cosmetic library fixes'. **Decision**: under the brain_only profile, the brain process loads an in-process ORT session using the CPU execution provider for query-time embedding. The session uses the same ONNX model artifact that the indexer uses for chunk vectorization (`runtime_embedding_snapshot_dir()/onnx/model.onnx`), guaranteeing vector-space coherence between query embeddings and persisted `Chunk.embedding` rows.

**Context**: Decided 2026-05-02 in response to the user (Didier) observation that without this path, the indexer's vectorization budget produces no semantic retrieval benefit on brain_only deployments — the persisted embeddings are dead weight from the brain's perspective. The brain literally would not deserve its name without semantic retrieval over its own canonical vectors.

**Rationale**:
- DuckDB is the right backend (DEC-AXO-034 confirmed TensorRT qualification supersedes CUDA optimization for indexer; the storage decision for embeddings landed on DuckDB's native FLOAT[DIMENSION] columns + `array_cosine_distance` already used in `tools_dx.rs:607`).
- The bottleneck is exclusively the absence of a query-time embedding path under brain_only, not anything about the storage or distance computation.
- ORT already supports CPU as a first-class execution provider; the existing `OrtGpuFirstTextEmbedding::try_new(lane, idx, use_cuda=false)` constructor (`gpu_backend.rs:130`) loads a CPU session with the same model + tokenizer + I/O binding that the GPU path uses.
- CPU latency for a typical 384-dim embedding model on 8-core CPU: 10–80 ms for a single query. This fits within MCP timeout budgets and comfortably below human perception threshold.
- Memory cost: ~100–200 MB RAM for the ORT session + model weights — negligible compared to the brain's existing footprint.

**Alternatives considered and rejected**:
- **Subprocess CPU embedding** (option E3): isolates memory but adds IPC complexity and serialization overhead. The ~150 MB RAM saving is not worth the complexity in this case.
- **External embedding API** (e.g., Voyage AI, OpenAI): adds network latency, breaks autonomous operation, contradicts the self-contained ethos of Axon (PIL-AXO-001 Shared Runtime Truth).
- **Pre-warmed embedding cache** (cache common queries): too narrow a scope, doesn't generalize, doesn't help novel queries.
- **Skip the chunk-LIKE fallback under brain_only** (option A1, originally proposed): treats the symptom (timeout) but amputates a working code path AND fails to address the deeper architectural question raised by the user. Rejected after the user clarified the question.
- **Quick-fix DuckDB query timeout** (option F): masks the symptom without fixing the architecture. Rejected for the same reason.

**Trade-offs**:
- Slight increase in brain process boot time (loading the ORT session is ~1–2s on first start). Mitigation: the implementation may load lazily on first query if startup time becomes a concern.
- Slight increase in steady-state RAM (~100–200 MB). Acceptable.
- Query latency is now CPU-bound under brain_only, not GPU-bound. For latency-critical paths a future indexer-collocated brain can opt into the GPU subprocess (already supported); brain_only deployments accept the CPU latency in exchange for not running a GPU.

**Consequences**:
- REQ-AXO-087 ("distinguish profile-excluded vs transient") becomes partially obsolete — brain_only no longer excludes semantic search by profile design; it now serves it via CPU. The "transient" wording stays for indexer profiles when their worker is starting up. The "profile-excluded" wording is retired.
- REQ-AXO-124 part 2 (multi-token slow timeout) is naturally resolved — the slow chunk-LIKE fallback is no longer the only retrieval path under brain_only; the semantic path now finds matches first.
- REQ-AXO-106 (IST projection freshness label) remains accurate; this decision does not change the freshness contract.
- Future REQ to track: activate DuckDB VSS extension once E1 is in place to give sub-linear k-NN over Chunk.embedding.

**Decision**: Axon runtime adopts a subsystem-tagged tristate readiness contract (Ready / Degraded { reason } / Failed { reason }) replacing the de-facto binary 'rising / down' signal currently emitted by start.sh and the latent `truth_status: canonical|degraded` field in mcp__axon__status. Each subsystem reports its own state into a thread-safe registry; an overall runtime readiness is computed as a roll-up. See CPT-AXO-023 for the architectural pattern.

**Context**: Decided 2026-05-02 to address REQ-AXO-098 (degraded-readiness contract) and unblock REQ-AXO-097 (watchdog) and REQ-AXO-094 (BEAM alarm classification) which both depend on having a structured per-subsystem state to escalate to. Validated by user (Didier) after rejecting a global tristate (option A, too coarse) and a separate health endpoint (option C, duplicates status).

**Rationale**:
- Granularity is the whole point: a single `overall` flag does not let 097 know which role to restart, and does not let 094 project a memory alarm onto a specific subsystem.
- Roll-up rule (Failed > Degraded > Ready) is unambiguous and matches operator expectations.
- The reporter pattern matches the existing `degraded_notes` vec but replaces a free-form string list with structured `(subsystem, state, reason)` triples that downstream tools can act on.
- No new MCP tool: extends the existing `status` surface so the contract stays in one place.

**Alternatives rejected**:
- **A. Global tristate only**: simple, ~150 LOC. Rejected because 097/094 cannot be implemented cleanly on top of it.
- **C. Separate `health` MCP tool**: Kubernetes-style; ~300 LOC. Rejected because it duplicates `status` for marginal gain and adds surface area.
- **D. Minimal rename** of existing `truth_status`. Rejected because it does not unblock 097/094.

**Trade-offs**:
- ~250 LOC + tests for B vs ~150 LOC for A. The extra investment buys 097/094 their prerequisite contract.
- Subsystem identity stabilization: the initial roster (brain_mcp, ist_writer, ist_reader, dashboard, embedder, watcher) becomes a stable contract; future subsystems must be added explicitly with their own reporter.
- Backward compatibility: the legacy `truth_status` and `degraded_notes` fields are preserved as aliases so existing MCP consumers keep working.

**Consequences**:
- REQ-AXO-097 (watchdog) becomes implementable: it watches subsystem `last_observed_at_ms` and flips a subsystem to Failed when no recent update is seen, and (separately) restarts the role process.
- REQ-AXO-094 (BEAM alarm classification) becomes implementable: the dashboard application subscribes to `:alarm_handler`, projects each alarm onto a subsystem state update (memory → dashboard:degraded(memory_pressure); disk → ist_writer:degraded(disk_almost_full)), and emits an MCP-side telemetry event so the runtime registry sees it.
- start.sh's 'Axon is rising' message is gated on overall=Ready; reaches 'Axon is DEGRADED: ...' or 'Axon FAILED to start: ...' otherwise. The mismatch flagged in REQ-AXO-095 (where 'rising' was emitted on a broken runtime) is structurally prevented.
- Customer-facing 'is it healthy' becomes a one-call answer with structured machine-actionable detail; LLM operators can decide which subsystem to act on without parsing prose.

Observed 2026-05-03: function `dev_baseline_wait_for_indexer_measurement_window` in `scripts/lib/dev-baseline.sh` greps the indexer status output for SIX markers:
  - `STATUS  HEALTHY`
  - `brain_ready=false`
  - `indexer_ready=true`
  - `process_role=indexer`
  - `ist_writer_authority=indexer`
  - `ist_snapshot_state=fresh`

The last 5 markers are MCP-derived (data.runtime_authority.* fields surfaced via `mcp__axon__status`). In an indexer-only baseline (which is what cold-reset starts), MCP brain is NOT running, so these fields cannot be queried, and `bash scripts/lib/status-indexer.sh` does NOT print them. The wait function then loops 240s and returns rc=1.

Downstream: callers like `qualify_runtime.py` and `benchmark-vector-token-matrix.sh` see `cold baseline reset failed (rc=1)` even though the indexer is healthy and producing work.

Fix options:
1. Replace the marker grep with telemetry-derived signals readable from the indexer's own runtime-heartbeat.json or telemetry socket.
2. OR change the cold-reset to spin up brain too (split mode) so MCP markers are available — simpler but doubles startup time.

Acceptance criteria:
1. `dev_baseline_wait_for_indexer_measurement_window` succeeds on a healthy indexer-only runtime within ~30s.
2. No dependency on MCP fields when indexer-only baseline.
3. Existing split-mode wait (`dev_baseline_wait_for_stable_measurement_window`) keeps working unchanged.

Tags: axon-bug, benchmark, deliverability, blocker
Priority: high
Originator: Claude Opus 4.7 session 2026-05-03 (paired with REQ-AXO-166 — the script bug surfaces this fragile contract)

## Scope

Acceptance #1 measurement for DEC-AXO-072 Phase A (write-through batched hot status cache). Goal: prove the in-memory cache reaches >=60 ch/s mean e2e on Axon repo, validating REQ-AXO-187 hypothesis that 70-85% of throughput loss was attributable to FVQ ops.

## Setup

- Branch: `feat/AXO-181-simplify-vector-lane` HEAD `5c4de82` (J.4) on top of `4dfeae6` (J.3), `6f90efb` (J.2), `a4729e7` (J.1).
- Build: `cargo build --bins` inside devenv shell.
- Stop+wipe: `./scripts/axon-dev stop --hard; rm -rf .axon-dev/graph_v2`.
- Start: same env as VAL-AXO-032 reproducer **plus** `AXON_HOT_STATUS_CACHE_ENABLED=true`.
- Verified env propagation: indexer process env shows `AXON_HOT_STATUS_CACHE_ENABLED=true`.
- Probe: 30s warm-up + 12 samples x 15s = 180s window. CSV `dev-probe-val033-cache-20260505T164828Z.csv`.

## Throughput evidence

| Phase | Mean ch/s | Peak ch/s |
|---|---|---|
| Warm-up (30s) | ~43 (1294 chunks) | -- |
| Probe (165s, 11 samples) | **22.87** | 39.6 (sample 4) |
| Probe excluding 2 zero windows | 27.4 | 39.6 |
| Total chunks at probe end | 5585 | -- |

Compared to VAL-AXO-032 baseline (commit G + H.1, NO cache):

| Mode | Probe mean | Total chunks |
|---|---|---|
| Baseline (no cache) | 23.9 ch/s | 5805 |
| Phase A (cache on) | **22.87 ch/s** | 5585 |
| Delta | **-4.3% (within noise)** | -3.8% |

## Acceptance status

- **DEC-AXO-072 acceptance #1 (>=60 ch/s mean over 180s):** NOT met. 22.87 vs 60 = 38% of target.
- **Acceptance #2 (round-trip equivalence):** met. FVQ rows correctly produced via flush thread; mark_done DELETEs cleanly; no orphan rows observed.
- **Acceptance #3 (boot hydration):** working. Indexer started with empty FVQ + populated cache via graph projection writes through cache.mark_ready.
- **Acceptance #5 (no regression on lib tests):** met (929/0/2).
- **Acceptance #6 (bit-identical when cache disabled):** met (cache OFF = direct DB path, validated by all preceding tests).

## Hypothesis falsified

REQ-AXO-187 attributed 70-85% of throughput loss to FVQ ops based on **latency math extrapolation** (not direct profiling). VAL-AXO-033 demonstrates this attribution was incorrect. Eliminating the FVQ JOIN claim and the per-claim FVQ UPDATE produces ZERO measurable throughput change.

## Revised bottleneck analysis

The actual hot path per vector-lane iteration:

1. Cache claim (sub-microsecond): saved ~5-10ms vs DB JOIN -> **~0.2-0.4 ms/chunk saved at 24 chunks/iteration**
2. mark_started UPDATE File (sync, cold field): ~5ms (unchanged)
3. **prepare_vector_embed_batch**: SELECTs Chunk content from DB -> **likely 5-15ms per batch** (untouched by cache)
4. **embed_prepared_batch_with_breakdown** (GPU): ~20ms / 24 chunks (= 0.8ms/chunk) (model-bound)
5. **update_chunk_embeddings INSERT** (96KB durable+fsync): **~30-50ms / batch** (unchanged by cache)
6. mark_done DELETE FVQ: ~5ms (still synchronous, cache evicts only)

Dominant costs (>50% of per-iteration time):
- ChunkEmbedding INSERT (durable, 96KB payload, fsync) — Phase A does not touch this
- prepare_vector_embed_batch (Chunk SELECT) — Phase A does not touch this

## Decision gate (DEC-AXO-072)

Per the validation plan: **>=60 ch/s -> Phase A SUFFICES; 30-60 ch/s -> Phase B justified; <30 ch/s -> bottleneck elsewhere, Phase B will not help.**

Measured 22.87 ch/s mean (and 27.4 excluding zero windows) places this firmly in the **'<30 ch/s, bottleneck elsewhere'** band. **Phase B (write-back with WAL) MUST NOT proceed.** It would be ~400 LOC of crash-recovery complexity for the same null result.

## Recommendations

1. **Keep the cache infrastructure** as opt-in (env disabled by default). Cost: zero (already merged, dormant). Value: optional knob for future experiments; useful if upstream improvements (graph_projection parallelization) make FVQ ops the new hotpath.
2. **Open new investigation** for the actual bottleneck (REQ to be created by next session): direct profiling (`perf record` or custom tracing breakdown) to identify whether the dominant cost is ChunkEmbedding INSERT durability or graph_projection upstream feed rate.
3. **Prioritize candidates**:
   - Async ChunkEmbedding INSERT (vectors land durable but DELETEd from hot path) — risky, requires careful crash semantics
   - Parallelize graph_projection (currently single-threaded? check `AXON_GRAPH_WORKERS`)
   - Compress vector payload before INSERT (~30% size reduction estimated)
   - Switch ChunkEmbedding to a side-store (Parquet on disk) and reference by id -- structural redesign
4. Consider rolling back DEC-AXO-072 J.3+J.4 wiring if the cache remains opt-in indefinitely (J.1+J.2 module + flush thread can stay as pure infrastructure). Decision: operator.

## Evidence files

- CSV: `dev-probe-val033-cache-20260505T164828Z.csv`
- Heartbeat at probe end: `embedder_provider.effective=cuda`, `degraded_reason=null`, `vector_chunks_embedded_total=5585`.
- Lib tests post-J.4: 929/0/2.

## Originator

Claude Opus 4.7 V3 + Didier (2026-05-05 ~19:00 UTC). Honest measurement; hypothesis falsified.

## Tags

performance, vector-pipeline, falsified-hypothesis, partial-analysis, axon-product-improvement

## Links (intended)

- VERIFIES DEC-AXO-072 (decision gate -> Phase B not justified)
- VERIFIES REQ-AXO-187 (the 70-85% DB attribution is wrong per direct measurement)

## Context

VAL-AXO-034 + diagnostic profiling 2026-05-05 demonstrate that DuckDB write performance degrades geometrically (commit_ms 132ms -> 22429ms over 80s) under our vector pipeline workload. The plugin (`src/axon-plugin-duckdb/src/lib.rs`) is a thin wrapper around the Rust `duckdb` crate; it adds no overhead. The bottleneck is intrinsic to DuckDB column-store under high-frequency writes of FLOAT[1024] arrays + WAL fsync amplification.

DEC-AXO-072 (hot status cache, 4 commits) and K.1 (cap batch=20 + bg checkpoint) both produced no measurable throughput improvement (24-25 ch/s sustained). The system is stuck at ~17% of GPU peak (140 ch/s).

Web research 2026-05-05 confirmed:
- Memgraph's `migrate.duckdb()` procedure can read FROM DuckDB and Parquet/S3/Iceberg/Delta Lake
- DuckPGQ extension brings SQL/PGQ graph queries to DuckDB itself (community extension)
- The 2026 ecosystem is aligned around DuckDB + Parquet + side-stores for high-write workloads

Memgraph in our codebase (`bin_memgraph_publication.rs` 1186 LOC) is a SEPARATE binary, NOT in the indexer hot path; it consumes a frozen DuckDB snapshot for human visualization only. So Memgraph is not our bottleneck.

## Decision

Introduce a **Parquet side-store** for `ChunkEmbedding` (the FLOAT[1024] vector arrays). DuckDB remains canonical for the relational structure (Symbol, Chunk, CONTAINS, CALLS, FVQ, File, FileLifecycleEvent). At MCP query time, DuckDB joins back to the Parquet store via `parquet_scan()`. Memgraph publication path is unchanged — still reads from the frozen DuckDB snapshot which now references Parquet via foreign keys.

## Architecture

```
DuckDB (canonical, lean):
  - Symbol (with optional embedding column kept for legacy callers per DEC-AXO-070 commit G)
  - Chunk (id, source_type, source_id, project_code, file_path, kind, content, content_hash, ...)
  - ChunkEmbedding (chunk_id, model_id, source_hash, embedded_at_ms, parquet_partition)  <- NO `embedding` column
  - CONTAINS, CALLS, CALLS_NIF, ...
  - FileVectorizationQueue, File, FileLifecycleEvent
  - SOLL.* (unchanged)

Parquet side-store (.axon-{live,dev}/graph_v2/embeddings/):
  - Partition strategy: hourly or per-N-chunks rolling files
    embeddings/2026-05-05T17/part-00001.parquet
    embeddings/2026-05-05T17/part-00002.parquet
  - Schema: chunk_id (VARCHAR), model_id (VARCHAR), embedding (FLOAT[1024]), source_hash (VARCHAR), embedded_at_ms (BIGINT)
  - Append-only writes; never UPDATE in place
  - Compaction policy: merge small files into bigger ones during off-peak (later phase)

Vector lane write path:
  1. embed batch -> Vec<(chunk_id, source_hash, embedding)>
  2. Append to current Parquet partition file (single fsync at file close, not per row)
  3. INSERT chunk_id metadata into ChunkEmbedding DuckDB table (no array data, fast)
  4. mark_done DELETE FVQ

Brain MCP retrieve_context query path:
  - Existing: SELECT ... FROM ChunkEmbedding WHERE ... -> reads from DuckDB column store
  - New: SELECT ... FROM ChunkEmbedding ce JOIN parquet_scan('.axon/graph_v2/embeddings/**.parquet') p ON ce.chunk_id = p.chunk_id WHERE ...
  - DuckDB optimizer pushes predicates into parquet_scan; reads only matching files
  - For hot retrievals (recent embeddings), DuckDB caches Parquet metadata + reads relevant column blocks
```

## What we KEEP

- DuckDB as canonical store (relational data + FK to Parquet)
- DEC-AXO-070 single-thread vector lane
- DEC-AXO-071 split + inline paths (ChunkEmbedding write path is the same regardless)
- Memgraph publication contract (binary still reads DuckDB snapshot; Parquet path is transparent via DuckDB views)
- Hot status cache infrastructure (DEC-AXO-072 J.1+J.2, opt-in)
- All existing CHUNK_EMBEDDING_MODEL_ID, DIMENSION constants

## What we REMOVE/REPLACE

- `ChunkEmbedding.embedding FLOAT[1024]` column from DuckDB schema -> migrated to Parquet
- `update_chunk_embeddings(model_id, &[(chunk_id, source_hash, embedding)])` body: replace SQL VALUES building with Parquet append + DuckDB metadata INSERT
- `INSERT OR REPLACE INTO ChunkEmbedding (chunk_id, model_id, embedding, source_hash, embedded_at_ms)` SQL -> two-step: 1. append to Parquet partition, 2. INSERT chunk_id metadata

## Phased delivery plan

**Phase L.1 — Parquet writer module (~150 LOC)**
- Add crate dep: `parquet = "54"`, `arrow = "54"` (or duckdb's parquet writer if accessible)
- New module `src/axon-core/src/embedder/parquet_embedding_store.rs`
- Append-only writer with rolling files (1MB or 10000 rows per partition file)
- Atomic file finalization (write to .tmp, rename on close)
- Boot-time scan to discover existing partition files
- Tests: round-trip 1000 vectors, partition rollover, recovery from .tmp orphans

**Phase L.2 — Schema migration + dual-write (~80 LOC)**
- Add `parquet_partition VARCHAR` column to ChunkEmbedding via ALTER TABLE
- Modify `update_chunk_embeddings` to: 1. append to Parquet, 2. INSERT metadata (no embedding array)
- Add migration path: existing ChunkEmbedding.embedding rows stay readable via the old column; new writes go to Parquet
- env flag `AXON_PARQUET_EMBEDDING_STORE_ENABLED=true|false` (default false initially)

**Phase L.3 — Read path: parquet_scan integration (~100 LOC)**
- Modify `retrieve_context` queries to JOIN ChunkEmbedding with `parquet_scan(...)` when parquet_partition IS NOT NULL
- DuckDB query planner handles Parquet pruning automatically
- Validate retrieve_context still returns correct results vs old path
- Tests: golden snapshot of retrieve_context output before/after migration

**Phase L.4 — Default flip + measurement (~10 LOC)**
- env flag default flipped to `true`
- Dev e2e probe with full Axon repo
- Capture VAL-AXO-035: throughput delta vs baseline
- Acceptance: >=80 ch/s mean (3x improvement over current ~25 ch/s)

**Phase L.5 — Drop DuckDB embedding column (later, ~20 LOC)**
- After all reads validated against Parquet path
- ALTER TABLE ChunkEmbedding DROP COLUMN embedding (DuckDB supports this)
- Old column data archived but no longer read

## Acceptance criteria

1. **Throughput**: Phase L.4 reaches >=80 ch/s mean e2e on Axon repo (3x baseline 25 ch/s).
2. **Read parity**: `retrieve_context` returns identical results before/after migration (golden tests).
3. **commit_ms flat**: Writer Actor commit_ms stays <500ms over 90s probe (no progressive degradation).
4. **Crash safety**: kill -9 indexer mid-batch, restart -> no data loss for committed chunks (.tmp files cleaned up at boot).
5. **Memgraph compatibility**: existing memgraph-publication binary continues to work (reads ChunkEmbedding via DuckDB views; Parquet pruning is transparent).
6. **Backward compat**: rollback via `AXON_PARQUET_EMBEDDING_STORE_ENABLED=false` falls back to DuckDB-only (until Phase L.5).
7. **No regression on lib tests** (929/0/2 baseline).

## Anti-patterns explicitly avoided

- Do NOT delete the DuckDB ChunkEmbedding table outright. Keep it as the metadata index + FK to Parquet.
- Do NOT add a separate query API for vectors. retrieve_context API is unchanged.
- Do NOT introduce a new vector store query language. SQL via DuckDB parquet_scan() is the contract.
- Do NOT add per-tenant or per-project Parquet sharding in Phase L.1-L.4. One process-wide partition tree.
- Do NOT compact partitions in the hot path. Add as Phase L.6+ later if needed.
- Do NOT migrate Symbol or Chunk content to Parquet in this DEC. Vectors only.

## Risks

| Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| parquet_scan() join slow for retrieve_context | DuckDB optimizer pushes predicates; partition pruning effective if chunk_id ordering aligns with time |
| Crash mid-Parquet write corrupts file | .tmp file pattern + atomic rename; orphans cleaned at boot |
| Parquet small files explosion | Rollover at 10000 rows or 1MB; later phase compacts |
| Two stores out of sync | DuckDB ChunkEmbedding row inserted ONLY after Parquet append + close confirmed |
| Memgraph publication breaks | Memgraph reads via DuckDB views; Parquet is transparent |
| Brain process can't read Parquet | DuckDB binary loads parquet extension by default; verified in plugin init |

## Why this design over alternatives

| Alt | Why rejected |
|---|---|
| DuckDB Appender API | Bypass SQL parser cost, but doesn't fix column-store re-encoding for FLOAT[1024]; gain unclear |
| RocksDB side-store | Adds C++ dep; Parquet ecosystem fit better with Memgraph migrate.duckdb() |
| Sqlite side-store | OLTP-friendly but vector arrays still expensive; Parquet append cheaper |
| Stay on DuckDB + tune | Multiple tuning attempts (DEC-AXO-072 K.1, 64MB checkpoint) showed no fix |
| In-memory + lazy write | Crash loses unembedded vectors; reload all at boot is slow at scale |

REQ-AXO-066 Phase 2 partial scope discovered 2026-05-03: the hot-path delta path (`stage_hot_path_delta_count`) already path-routes via `Scanner::project_code_for_path(store, path)` when `project_code` is empty (fs_watcher.rs:394). The buffered/single-file delta path (`enqueue_single_file_delta`) does NOT — it rejects with reason `missing_explicit_project_context_for_buffered_delta` (fs_watcher.rs:588-596). Result: a global watcher with `project_code=""` cannot multi-tenant route buffered events.

=== Architectural reason for the asymmetry ===
By design the `enqueue_*` family is decoupled from `GraphStore` so the watcher hot path stays I/O-free at event time; resolution happens at flush-time. `IngressFileEvent.project_code` is `String` (not `Option<String>`) and is set at event-creation time, so any path-routing must resolve before the event hits the buffer.

=== Phase 2 closure work (~300 LOC, multi-callsite) ===
1. Add `store: Option<&GraphStore>` parameter to `enqueue_single_file_delta`, `enqueue_hot_path_delta_count`, and the public `enqueue_hot_delta_with_guard` wrapper.
2. When `scanner.project_code` is empty AND `store.is_some()`, call `scanner.project_code_for_path(store, &absolute)` instead of rejecting.
3. When both are empty, keep the existing reject-with-probe path.
4. Update all 5+ callers of these functions to forward the store ref.
5. Smoke test: dual-project watch root (subdir A registered as PJA, subdir B registered as PJB), buffered file event in each, assert each event lands in `IngressFileEvent.project_code` matching its subtree's registered project.

=== Acceptance criteria ===
- `enqueue_single_file_delta` accepts `Option<&GraphStore>` and resolves project_code via path when explicit code is empty
- Existing single-project tests still pass
- New multi-project smoke test passes
- `IngressFileEvent.project_code` continues to be `String` (no schema change to avoid downstream breakage)
- DEC-AXO-064 Q2=D fully delivered

=== Why not done in REQ-AXO-066 Phase 1 session ===
Phase 1 closed the SOLL/IST `project_code` partitioning gap (commit c9041da). Phase 2 was attempted in the same session but the watcher event path's I/O-free design made the threading change touch many functions; deferred to a fresh session with planning to keep the change atomic.

Reproducible failure observed 2026-05-03 16:46-16:55. Both the v0.8.0-152 (live) and v0.8.0-154 (newly-built) axon-brain binaries panicked at startup with:

  FATAL INTERNAL Error: Failed to append to PRIMARY_Edge_0: Constraint Error: PRIMARY KEY or UNIQUE constraint violation: duplicate key "REQ-AXO-149, PIL-AXO-002, BELONGS_TO"

The brain process exited with code 134 (SIGABRT). Live did not reach Ready; promote_live_safe.sh's restart phase failed; live ran in indexer-graph mode (REQ-AXO-150) leaving MCP unavailable.

Root-cause hypothesis: the documented multi-tenant backfill (CLAUDE memory: `soll.Edge ... carry a denormalized project_code column (backfilled to 'AXO' on first boot for legacy rows; edges inherit from source Node when known)`) is implemented as INSERT-and-fail rather than ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE / SET project_code = ... WHERE project_code IS NULL. The 3 edges I created today via `soll_manager link` (REQ-AXO-149/150/151 → PIL-AXO-002/004/001 BELONGS_TO) carried project_code = NULL. On next brain boot, the backfill tried to recreate them and hit the (source_id, target_id, relation_type) primary key.

Recovery applied today: stop live, run `UPDATE main.Edge SET project_code='AXO' WHERE project_code IS NULL AND source_id IN ('REQ-AXO-149', 'REQ-AXO-150', 'REQ-AXO-151')` directly on `.axon/graph_v2/soll.db` via the duckdb CLI, then start brain (✅ reaches Ready). This is a workaround — the underlying create-link path must populate project_code at INSERT time so no NULL rows are ever written.

Customer-facing impact: any operator who creates a `soll_manager link` (the documented standing protocol step for SOLL hygiene per CPT-AXO-019) will leave a NULL row that bricks the next brain boot. This is a customer-DELIVERABILITY blocker.

Acceptance criteria:
1. `soll_manager` link path always populates `project_code` from the source Node (or rejects the call if it cannot).
2. Backfill on first boot uses `UPDATE ... WHERE project_code IS NULL` (or the equivalent), never `INSERT`. Idempotent across reboots.
3. Regression test: create a link via MCP, restart brain, assert brain reaches Ready in <60s.
4. SOLL self-validation flags any existing NULL-project_code rows in `soll.Edge` so contributors can fix them before bricking live.

Backlog — today every promotion that updates `bin/axonctl` requires stopping live (the running `axonctl supervise` keeps the old binary in memory until restart). The downtime window is currently shared with `axon-brain` + `axon-indexer` restart, so adding axonctl to the cycle (REQ-AXO-153) doesn't change the user-visible blast radius. But long-term goal: zero-downtime supervisor swap.

Pattern: SIGUSR1 handler triggers `execve(<new_binary>, argv, envp)` while keeping owned FDs (tmux master, telemetry socket, IST/SOLL writer guards). Pre-existing patterns in nginx, unicorn, haproxy.

This is non-trivial — axonctl owns child runtime processes (axon-brain, axon-indexer) and writer guards. The supervisor handoff must preserve those without orphaning children.

Not blocking REQ-AXO-153 (which uses the existing restart-with-downtime window). File for fresh-budget design session.

Observed 2026-05-03: `promote_live_safe.sh --project AXO` shipped `axon-brain` and `axon-indexer` v0.8.0-157 to live, but `bin/axonctl` stayed at v0.8.0-13-g5ac944d-dirty (months old). REQ-AXO-151's axonctl-side role_contract_violations field is therefore correctly compiled and committed but inert in production until the supervising axonctl is itself re-deployed.

Root cause: `create_release_manifest.sh` enumerates `axon-brain` + `axon-indexer` artifacts only. `promote_live.sh` line 142-160 iterates over `manifest.artifacts.items()` and copies each to `bin/<name>`, so the iteration is already generic — adding `axonctl` to the manifest makes it ship without further changes to promote_live.

Downstream gap (separate REQ): the running `axonctl supervise` daemon loads its binary at exec() time. Even after `bin/axonctl` is updated, the running supervisor keeps the old code in memory until restarted. Because axonctl supervises the runtime, restarting it implies stopping live (downtime). This is acceptable as the existing `--restart-live` flow already restarts brain + indexer; we just codify axonctl in the same window. A graceful re-exec (SIGUSR1 + execve preserving FDs) is filed as REQ-AXO-154 backlog.

Acceptance criteria:
1. `create_release_manifest.sh` lists `axonctl` in the artifacts dict alongside `axon-brain` / `axon-indexer`, with sha256 + path + build_info_path.
2. `promote_live.sh` (no change required, artifacts loop is generic) deploys `bin/axonctl` and `bin/axonctl.build-info` from the new artifact during restart.
3. After a clean `promote_live_safe.sh --project AXO`, `cat bin/axonctl.build-info` shows the just-built BUILD_ID, not a stale one.
4. Post-promotion `axonctl status --json` exposes `role_contract_violations` (the REQ-AXO-151 field) — proof the new axonctl code is running.
5. SOLL self-validation flags `bin/axonctl.build-info` more than N hours older than `bin/axon-brain.build-info` as a release-pipeline drift warning.

Originally surfaced as 'Axon security surface' but reformulated to keep Axon scoped: cargo-audit, gitleaks, semgrep, OWASP regex SAST belong in CI tooling — NOT in Axon. The actual Axon-native gap is a **structural invariants validator** that lets a project declare graph-level rules on the IST and have Axon enforce them.

Example rules a user could declare:
- 'Every path from a Symbol with kind=user_input to Symbol.name=execute_param MUST traverse a Symbol.name=escape_sql.'
- 'No Symbol with is_unsafe=true may be reached from a public_api boundary without an attached SOLL Decision tagged unsafe-justified.'
- 'Every Symbol with kind=mutation must have at least one CALLS edge from a kind=test.'

This IS Axon's domain because it requires graph traversal + Symbol.id contract + SOLL annotation linkage — the exact substrate that already exists in IST. Outside the line: anything that needs taint flow analysis at the AST/dataflow level (semgrep), dependency vulnerability databases (cargo-audit), or git-history secret scanning (gitleaks). Those tools call Axon for graph context, not the reverse.

Acceptance criteria:
1. New MCP tool `validate_invariants(project_code, rule_set?)` returns per-rule pass/fail + counter-example paths from the IST.
2. Rule format: declarative DSL or cypher-templated, persisted as SOLL Concept entities so they survive across sessions.
3. Built-in rule library covers the 3 example shapes above as starter templates.
4. `axon_pre_flight_check` integrates rule validation as a soft gate (warns, does not block) until rules graduate to hard contracts.
5. Per-rule results carry severity + remediation_hint so an LLM caller can act.

**Pre-implementation SOTA evaluation required**: survey existing structural-rule frameworks (CodeQL queries, semgrep cross-file rules, depgraph, archlint), identify the shape that maps cleanest to IST + cypher, and document why Axon's adoption is differentiated rather than re-implementation. Write the eval as a `decision` SOLL node before any code.

CPT-AXO-019 standing protocol = OBSERVE → LOG → LINK → REPLAN → EXECUTE. The LINK step requires `soll_attach_evidence` on each REQ a commit closes. Today this is a manual call after `axon_commit_work`. Friction observed live in this 2026-05-03 session: I had to call `soll_attach_evidence` six times in a row (REQ-AXO-149/150/151/152/153/155/156) with verbatim commit SHAs that the server already had during commit.

`axon_commit_work` already inspects: commit message, diff_paths, post-commit SHA. Adding 'parse `REQ-AXO-NNN` mentions from commit message body → auto-call internal soll_attach_evidence with artifact_type=validation, artifact_ref=commit:<sha>, summary=first sentence of commit body' is purely plumbing.

Proposed scope:
1. After successful commit, parse message body with regex `\b(REQ|DEC|CPT|VAL)-[A-Z]{3}-\d{3}\b` — gather distinct entity IDs.
2. For each ID, call internal `soll_attach_evidence` with: artifact_type=validation, artifact_ref=commit:<short-sha>, summary=truncated first paragraph.
3. Return the auto-attached count in `axon_commit_work` response.data.evidence_attached.
4. Idempotent: if same (entity, commit-sha) pair already exists in soll.main.Traceability, skip without error.
5. Optional opt-out via `auto_attach_evidence: false` arg for edge cases where the commit body shouldn't auto-link.

Why Axon-native: SOLL is canonical to Axon (PIL-AXO-003). axon_commit_work is the gateway. Bridging them removes ~90% of CPT-AXO-019 step-3 friction.

**Pre-implementation SOTA evaluation required**: survey commit-to-issue auto-linking patterns — GitHub/GitLab smart commits, conventional commits + commitlint, Sourcegraph code-intel, Linear+commit syntax. Pick the regex/format that minimizes false-positives (e.g., `Closes REQ-AXO-149` vs. bare `REQ-AXO-149` mention). Document the chosen syntax in CPT-AXO-019 update.

Acceptance criteria:
1. Commit body containing `REQ-AXO-NNN` triggers auto-attach with commit SHA evidence.
2. Idempotent on retry / re-commit.
3. Response payload exposes `evidence_attached: ["REQ-AXO-NNN", ...]`.
4. Opt-out arg honored.
5. SOTA eval committed as Decision (chosen syntax: prefix verb required vs. bare ID).
6. CPT-AXO-019 updated to document the auto-attach contract.

Observed 2026-05-03: first call to `soll_attach_evidence` used `ref` instead of `artifact_ref` and got rejected with 'each artifact needs an `artifact_ref` (or `path` / `file_path` / `uri` alias)'. The error is helpful but I had to manually rebuild the payload with the corrected key. For an LLM, that's an extra round-trip.

REQ-AXO-139 introduced `parameter_repair` as the universal contract for argument-validation errors. The current shape lists the invalid field + valid alternatives. Proposed extension: also include `corrected_payload`, the smallest legal mutation of the input that the server can confidently produce.

Example:
```json
{
  "parameter_repair": {
    "invalid_field": "artifacts[0].ref",
    "corrected_payload": { "artifact_type": "validation", "artifact_ref": "<your value>", "summary": "" }
  }
}
```

Semantics: server populates corrected_payload only when transformation is unambiguous (rename `ref` → `artifact_ref` because it's the documented alias). When ambiguous (missing required field with no default), corrected_payload is null and the LLM must supply.

Commercial framing: 'Axon reduces token cost for every integrated LLM — errors come back with the correct payload, not just a diagnostic. Measurable KPI: % of MCP retry-after-error sequences where the second call uses the auto-corrected payload verbatim.'

**Pre-implementation SOTA evaluation required**: survey error-with-correction patterns — GraphQL error extensions, gRPC error_details, Stripe API error.suggestion field, OpenAI function-calling self-repair examples. Identify the convention that minimizes LLM client code branching.

Acceptance criteria:
1. parameter_repair payload now includes optional `corrected_payload` field across MCP tools that already emit parameter_repair (REQ-AXO-139, REQ-AXO-145, REQ-AXO-148).
2. Field is null when correction is ambiguous; populated when unambiguous.
3. Test suite covers at least 5 representative cases (rename, type-coerce, default-fill, drop-extra, normalize-enum).
4. SOTA eval committed as Decision (chosen format + ambiguity rule).

`./scripts/axon status` currently reports `STATUS HEALTHY` even when the WARN line says `mcp socket missing (/tmp/axon-live-indexer-mcp.sock)`. For an instance whose role contract is to expose MCP, missing-MCP-socket is not a warning — it's a contract failure.

`architectural_drift` MCP tool exists today as a probe — a caller asks a question, gets a one-shot snapshot, moves on. The pillar PIL-AXO-001 (Shared Runtime Truth) and PIL-AXO-006 (Live-First Resource Governance) imply a stronger contract: drift should be a **continuously surfaced runtime signal**, not a thing operators remember to query. Proposed scope:
1. Live computation of a drift score per module on each indexer wave (or each `axon_commit_work` finalization) — reuses existing IST + architectural_drift logic.
2. Persisted history (`soll.main.ArchitecturalDriftHistory` or hot IST table) so trend lines exist.
3. Memgraph/dashboard view: heatmap by module, sparkline by score-over-time, top-N modules approaching threshold.
4. Threshold-crossing event (default: 95th percentile of recent history, configurable per project) emits a SOLL Validation entity automatically tagged `architectural_drift_threshold_exceeded` so it surfaces in the work plan.
5. `status` brief mentions any module currently above threshold as a `Current blocker:` entry (REQ-AXO-042 truth_cockpit channel).

Why Axon-native: every primitive already exists. Memgraph projection (DEC-AXO-035), runtime_observability, the architectural_drift tool, SOLL Validation entities. This is integration, not new product.

**Pre-implementation SOTA evaluation required**: survey continuous architecture-drift dashboards — NDepend, Sonargraph, code-maat, ArchUnit-Live, Tachi (post-2024). Identify which threshold heuristics work in practice (raw delta vs. EWMA vs. control-chart Z-score), which have actionable false-positive rates, and pick the algorithm before building the visualization.

Acceptance criteria:
1. Drift score per module persists across indexer cycles.
2. Dashboard view exposes heatmap + trend per module (Memgraph or Phoenix view).
3. Threshold crossing emits a SOLL Validation row with severity + module + score evidence.
4. `status` brief surfaces threshold-crossed modules in current_blocker when applicable.
5. SOTA eval committed as Decision (algorithm choice + threshold semantics).

Direct expression of DEC-AXO-040 (MCP-first project intelligence) and DEC-AXO-039 (MCP assistance score targets 95+). Today an LLM editing N files runs `cargo test` (or equivalent) blindly across the entire suite — minutes per cycle on a healthy suite, prohibitive on a slow one. Axon already knows: the diff blast radius (`impact`), the test coverage map (`Symbol.tested`), and the call/contains graph between symbols and tests.

New MCP tool `test_impact(diff_paths, project_code) → { minimal_test_set, confidence, fallback_recommendation }`:
- Walks `impact` upward from each touched symbol to find every test (Symbol.kind='test' or marked via `#[cfg(test)]` graph annotation) that transitively depends on the change.
- Returns ordered list of test paths (e.g., `cargo test --lib mcp::tests::context_and_analysis::test_axon_inspect`).
- Confidence label reflects coverage completeness: `high` when every touched symbol has a covering test in the IST; `medium` when partial; `low` (with fallback `run full suite`) when uncovered symbols exist.
- Optional `format=cargo|pytest|jest` for engine-specific invocation strings.

Commercial argument: this is the moment Axon stops being 'an intelligent probe layer' and becomes 'a measurable dev-loop accelerator'. Customers can quote 'cargo test cycle time divided by N' as the headline benefit.

**Pre-implementation SOTA evaluation required**: survey test-impact-analysis tooling — Microsoft Test Impact Analysis (Azure Pipelines), Bazel selective testing, Pants test selection, `cargo-nextest --filter`, Buck2 incremental testing, Facebook's 'AdmittanceTrace'. Identify which strategies reach >95% precision (no false-skip causing missed regression) at >70% recall (real test reduction). Document the algorithmic floor before coding the MCP tool.

Acceptance criteria:
1. MCP `test_impact(diff_paths, project_code)` returns ordered test list + confidence label.
2. Per-engine invocation strings (cargo / pytest / jest) for the LLM to copy-paste.
3. False-skip rate measured against held-out diffs from project history (regression test). Target: 0 missed-by-tool failures over the last 30 commits.
4. `axon_pre_flight_check` integrates `test_impact` recommendations as 'expected-to-be-run' set.
5. SOTA eval committed as Decision (chosen algorithm + precision/recall benchmark).

## Original root cause
Under dev `--indexer-full` + `gpu=avoid` policy, the canonical provider is `cpu`, but `runtime_boot.rs:319` unconditionally sets `AXON_GPU_EMBED_SERVICE_ENABLED=1` as a default. The original gate at `vector_worker_loop.rs:1033` checked only `gpu_embed_service_enabled()`, so the GPU subprocess was always spawned. The subprocess (after REQ-AXO-171 dispatch fix) immediately panicked on `dlopen` of `libonnxruntime.so` because `start.sh`'s `axon-ort-runtime.sh:37` only configures `ORT_DYLIB_PATH` + `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` when provider=cuda.

## Surprise outcome
The in-process CPU branch (`OrtGpuFirstTextEmbedding::try_new` with `force_gpu=false`) is succeeding in dlopening ORT, even though the start script doesn't explicitly configure it for CPU. Likely the runtime's tracing init or another code path patches `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` post-fork. Pipeline 2 throughput at end of 2-minute test: 71 chunks embedded out of ~1600 ready in queue (modest under `gpu=avoid` budget, expected).

## New design (accepted 2026-05-04)
Enrich `axon_init_project` kickoff bundle so a single MCP call gives the full session-bootstrap context — no new entity type needed.

### Kickoff bundle additions
1. **`in_progress_requirements`**: top-N (N=10) Requirements with `status='in_progress'` for the project, sorted by `metadata.updated_at` desc. Returned as `[{id, title, priority}]`.
2. **`wave_1_unblockers`**: cached top-3 from `soll_work_plan top=3` for the project. Returned as `[{id, type, title, score, descendants_unblocked}]`.
3. **`recent_req_commits`**: last 5 commits whose subject matches `REQ-[A-Z]+-\d+` for the project's git root. Returned as `[{sha, subject, age_seconds}]`. Skip silently if not a git repo.
4. **`recent_soll_writes`**: top-N (N=8) SOLL nodes for the project sorted by `metadata.updated_at` desc, regardless of type/status. Returned as `[{id, type, title, updated_at}]`.

### Session-as-Concept convention (kept as ad-hoc tool, not canonical type)
When an operator explicitly wants to capture session-local narrative beyond what the structured bundle provides, they create a `Concept` with `metadata.kind='session_summary'`. CPT-AXO-027 is the exemplar. This stays an opt-in pattern — not a required step.

## Acceptance criteria
1. `axon_init_project` returns the 4 new fields under `data.kickoff_bundle`. Empty arrays when no rows match.
2. Each field's payload is bounded (size limits documented) so a sparse project doesn't blow up the response.
3. Token cost vs current bundle: target < +500 tokens median (measured by `wc -c` on serialised response).
4. No SOLL schema migration. No new `EntityType` enum value. PIL-AXO-003's '9 canonical entity types' statement remains accurate.
5. Test: a project with 5 in_progress REQs + 10 recent commits returns the truncated bundle correctly.

In response to CPT-AXO-026 (pipeline complexity audit), validation will proceed by minimal-viable-test, not by full benchmark.

## Decision

Before any throughput / SOTA measurement, validate the pipeline on the **simplest possible scenario**:

1. **Single file scenario** — 1 file, ~50-200 tokens, NO concurrent graph projection load. The validation succeeds if exactly 1 ChunkEmbedding row is produced within 30s of vector worker readiness.
2. **Small batch scenario (10 files)** — isolated test project. Validates batch formation + scheduler activation without budget pressure.
3. **Mid-scale (1000 files)** — validates ready_queue stability, model warmup amortization.
4. **Full scale (~14k files)** — only after 1–3 succeed. Validates the production scenario.

Each step must produce a clear pass/fail with a single observable signal: `count(ChunkEmbedding) > N`.

## Anti-patterns explicitly rejected

- DO NOT debug the benchmark orchestration script (`benchmark-vector-token-matrix.sh`) before validating the underlying pipeline. Bugs in REQ-AXO-166/167/168 are real but downstream of this question.
- DO NOT seed dev from live. Use a SMALL focused test corpus (5-10 files in a tmpdir) so initial scan + budget pressure don't dominate.
- DO NOT increase budget envelopes to bypass the gate. Use a small enough corpus that no gate fires.
- DO NOT measure throughput before the binary success of step 1.

## Operational protocol per step

1. Stop dev clean.
2. Wipe `.axon-dev/graph_v2/*`.
3. Set `AXON_WATCH_DIR` and `AXON_PROJECTS_ROOT` to an isolated test corpus (one project root with N files).
4. Start dev `--indexer-full` (CPU embedder default; GPU only after step 3+ succeed).
5. Poll `count(ChunkEmbedding)` every 5s for 60s.
6. PASS if count > 0 within budget. FAIL otherwise — then inspect heartbeat to identify the blocking gate.

## Expected by-products of this methodology

- A test fixture (5-10 file tmpdir) that becomes the canonical Pipeline 2 smoke test.
- A gate-by-gate diagnostic checklist: "if no embedding, check gate 1–16 in this order".
- A documented MTTR for restoring Pipeline 2 to a producing state from any failed state.

## Why this Decision now

Session 2026-05-03 spent ~3 hours attempting to benchmark and never produced a single embedding. The corpus was too large, the budget gate fired, the bench-script orchestration bugs masked the symptom. A minimal-viable-test approach would have surfaced the budget gate in step 1, in <2 minutes.
