# Operating discipline for LLM-driven Axon sessions

Five rules to (a) minimize token waste, (b) prevent GPU resource
conflicts during Axon self-development, (c) preserve session memory
across context-window saturation.

## 1. Axon Live runtime mode by working context

| Working on | Live runtime | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Axon itself (this repo) | `--graph-only` or `--brain-only` | Free GPU for dev bench/qualify, keep IST graph queryable for navigation |
| Other projects (BKS, etc.) | full (indexer + vector) | No conflict; full search + embed available |

Trigger: when LLM detects MCP disconnected and `pwd` is the Axon repo,
run `./scripts/axon-live start --graph-only`. Otherwise full start.

## 2. NVML telemetry, never nvidia-smi (operator-validated)

`AXON_GPU_TELEMETRY_BACKEND=nvml` always. nvidia-smi readings
unreliable; NVML is accurate. VRAM ceiling 7 GB on 8 GB GPUs — above
that the driver spills allocations to system RAM and throughput
collapses (validated 2026-05-04: 2-worker bench dropped from
155 chunks/s single to 34 chunks/s dual on BGE-Large TensorRT,
matching ~4.5x RAM-spill ratio).

## 3. Conscious mental cache for SOLL IDs

Once a SOLL entity has been read in a session, do NOT re-fetch unless
an update is suspected. Reuse local recall of REQ/DEC/CPT content.
Token-saving rationale: `soll_query_context` costs 3-5 K tokens per
call; `cypher` 7-col projection costs ~300 tokens. Prefer cypher
over query_context when the target ID is already known.

## 4. SOLL writes optimized for future LLM consumption

Every SOLL entity description MUST be:
- English (project convention)
- Concise — no prose padding, no repetition
- Structured — headers, tables, bullets (not walls of text)
- Precise — canonical terms, exact MCP tool names, absolute file paths
- Self-contained — readable in isolation by a future LLM session

Anti-pattern: copy-pasting working-note markdown verbatim — rewrite as
structured spec. Each bloated description is paid for repeatedly when
re-loaded by future sessions; tight writing has compounding savings.

## 5. Context-window self-management

LLM agent monitors `[X% remaining]` markers in UserPromptSubmit
context. At ≤30% remaining (= 70% used) → emit checkpoint via
`axon_session_checkpoint` (REQ-AXO-176) and request session reset
rather than continuing into degraded zone.

## Why this is canonical (not advisory)

A typical multi-hour session emits ~50K tokens of MCP overhead. A
bloated 5K-token DEC re-loaded by 10 future sessions = 50K tokens
wasted. Tight discipline keeps Axon a net-positive memory tier vs.
raw LLM context. Without these rules, the structural intelligence
gain is offset by serialization cost.

## Validation evidence

- REQ-AXO-176 (session memory checkpoint cycle) — operationalises rule 5
- REQ-AXO-177 (L1 bench harness) — verified rule 1+2 throughput numbers
- Multi-worker collapse 2026-05-04 (155→34 chunks/s) — confirms rule 2 ceiling

Bit during REQ-AXO-171/173 validation (2026-05-03). After editing `runtime_boot.rs` at 19:59 and running `./scripts/axon-dev start --indexer-full`, the dev session ran the binary at `.axon/cargo-target/debug/axon-indexer` dated 18:45 — BEFORE my edit. The script does not invoke `cargo build` for the debug profile (only release builds happen via `start.sh:572` via `run_devenv_shell` for live promotion).

## Reproduction
1. Edit any `.rs` file under `src/axon-core/src/`
2. `./scripts/axon-dev start --indexer-full`
3. `stat -c '%y' .axon/cargo-target/debug/axon-indexer` shows OLDER than the edit

## Workaround
```bash
CARGO_TARGET_DIR=.axon/cargo-target cargo build --manifest-path src/axon-core/Cargo.toml --bin axon-indexer
./scripts/axon-dev start --indexer-full
```

## LLM trap impact
A fix verified by `cargo test --lib` will pass tests but the dev runtime will still exhibit the pre-fix behavior, making the LLM falsely conclude the fix doesn't work. ~30 minutes wasted in REQ-AXO-171 validation cycle re-checking the dispatch.

## Fix candidates
1. `scripts/axon-dev` (or `scripts/start.sh` dev branch): add a `cargo build --bin axon-indexer` step before tmux launch when `--instance dev`. Requires devenv shell context.
2. Document in CLAUDE.md / SKILL.md: "After editing Rust under axon-core, always rebuild dev binary before `axon-dev start`". Less robust but zero-cost.
3. Pre-flight check in `./scripts/axon-dev start`: stat-compare debug binary mtime vs newest .rs mtime under `src/axon-core/src/`; warn if stale.

## Triage (CPT-AXO-025)
Branch 3 — Commercial value-add (LLM-friction). The script works as documented but underperforms commercially because it makes iterative LLM-driven dev nearly impossible without explicit knowledge of the trap.

## Context

DEC-AXO-070 commit G (`540846b` 2026-05-05) split the pipeline into two single-purpose lanes:
- Pipeline #1 graph projection: watcher event → File row → AST parse → Symbol nodes + Chunks + relations → INSERT into DB → enqueue path into FileVectorizationQueue
- Pipeline #2 vector lane: claim queue → fetch chunks from DB → embed via BGE-Large/TensorRT → INSERT ChunkEmbedding

This split achieved DEC-AXO-070 acceptance #1 (>=30 ch/s e2e measured 30-70 ch/s sustained, peak 70). Operator directive 2026-05-05: keep this commit-G split as one path, but add a second "inline" path so the two architectures coexist for A/B experimentation over weeks.

## Decision

Add an **inline pipeline mode** gated by `AXON_VECTOR_PIPELINE_INLINE=true` (default false). In inline mode, graph projection workers embed chunks **before** the DB write, so `Chunk` and `ChunkEmbedding` rows land in the same `execute_batch` transaction. The `FileVectorizationQueue` insert is skipped.

Both modes share **one** BGE-Large model load via a channel-based GPU embedder service. No multi-worker GPU contention regardless of mode.

## Architecture

### Single shared GPU embedder service
- New module: `src/axon-core/src/embedder/inline_embed.rs` (~80 LOC)
  - Crossbeam channel: `static INLINE_TX: OnceLock<Sender<EmbedRequest>>`.
  - `pub fn embed_via_vector_lane(texts: Vec<String>) -> Result<Vec<Vec<f32>>>` blocks on response, returns vectors.
  - `pub fn inline_pipeline_enabled() -> bool` reads env.
  - `pub(crate) fn register_vector_lane_inbox(tx: Sender<...>)` called by vector_lane_worker at boot.

### Vector lane drains the inline channel between queue fetches
- Modify `src/axon-core/src/embedder/vector_worker_loop.rs` (~25 LOC):
  - At boot: create a (Sender, Receiver) pair, call `register_vector_lane_inbox`, keep the Receiver.
  - In the loop: BEFORE `fetch_pending_file_vectorization_work`, drain the Receiver via `try_recv` until empty, processing each request synchronously through the existing `model.embed_texts_with_breakdown` path. Send embeddings back via the request's response channel.
  - Inline requests have implicit priority (drained before queue fetch).

### Graph projection embeds inline when enabled
- Modify `src/axon-core/src/graph_ingestion.rs` (~70 LOC at the chunk-emission site, near line 549, and the post-execute_batch hook around line 854):
  - Capture `(chunk_id, content, content_hash, file_path)` tuples in parallel with `chunk_values` when `inline_pipeline_enabled()` is true.
  - After `execute_batch(&queries)` (chunks committed): for each file_vectorization_path,
    1. Collect texts in order.
    2. Call `embed_via_vector_lane(texts)`.
    3. Build `INSERT INTO ChunkEmbedding (chunk_id, model_id, embedding, source_hash) VALUES ...` for each (chunk_id, embedding, content_hash) triple.
    4. Build `UPDATE File SET vector_ready = TRUE WHERE path = ...`.
    5. Execute as a single batch.
  - When `inline_pipeline_enabled()` true: do NOT push into `FileVectorizationQueue` for those paths. The path is fully terminated inside the graph worker's transaction loop.
  - On `embed_via_vector_lane` failure: fall back to the queue (commit G path).

### Anti-patterns explicitly avoided
- **No graph workers loading BGE-Large directly.** That re-introduces multi-worker GPU OOM (REQ-AXO-181 step 4 cascade). The single-thread vector lane keeps owning the model.
- **No new SQL writer.** Inline INSERT ChunkEmbedding goes through the same writer connection as the chunk insert (sequential within `execute_batch`).
- **No new env knob beyond `AXON_VECTOR_PIPELINE_INLINE`.** REQ-AXO-184 just collapsed multi-knob complexity; do not re-introduce parallel switches.

## Implementation plan (split for context budget)

**H.1 — foundation (~80 LOC, no behavior change)**
- Create `inline_embed.rs` module.
- `vector_lane_worker` registers inbox + drains channel between fetches.
- Channel capacity bounded (`bounded(64)` to backpressure rather than unbound memory).
- Inline channel has zero senders by default → no behavior change for existing operators.
- Tests: unit test for channel round-trip via a stub model.

**H.2 — graph_ingestion inline path (~100 LOC)**
- Capture `(chunk_id, content, content_hash, file_path)` tuples alongside `chunk_values`.
- Post-execute_batch hook: when `inline_pipeline_enabled()` true, embed + write `ChunkEmbedding` + `File.vector_ready=TRUE`, skip queue insert.
- Fallback path on embed-channel timeout (>5s) or failure: enqueue normally (commit G path).
- Tests: integration test with a stub embedder asserting both modes produce identical end state on the same input.

## Acceptance criteria

1. **Both paths work side-by-side.** Setting `AXON_VECTOR_PIPELINE_INLINE=false` (default) reproduces commit G behavior exactly. Setting `=true` enables inline path.
2. **Single BGE-Large load** in either mode. `nvidia-smi` shows ~6 GB used, never more.
3. **Inline mode throughput >= split mode throughput** on the Axon repo. (Hypothesis: inline saves the chunk write+read roundtrip, so should be at least as fast.)
4. **No regression on commit G acceptance #1** (>=30 ch/s e2e in split mode).
5. **Lib tests pass** (916+/0/2 baseline preserved, +1-3 new tests for inline path).
6. **Failure mode safe.** Embed-channel timeout -> file falls back to queue path, no chunk loss.

## What we are explicitly NOT doing

- **Not** removing `FileVectorizationQueue`. It remains the canonical safety net.
- **Not** removing `vector_lane_worker`. It is the embedding service for both modes.
- **Not** changing `AXON_GRAPH_EMBEDDINGS_ENABLED=false` default. Graph workers still don't load BGE-Large directly.
- **Not** introducing a separate symbol embedding path. Symbols stay deferred per commit G (operator directive 2026-05-05).

## Why this design over alternatives

| Alt | Why rejected |
|---|---|
| Each graph worker loads BGE-Large | Multi-worker GPU OOM cascade (REQ-AXO-181 step 4) |
| New writer connection for ChunkEmbedding | DuckDB single-writer; new connection creates cross-tx race |
| Synchronous embed via DB queue priority bump | Doesn't actually inline; it's just a faster split flow |
| Drop the queue entirely | Removes safety net for embed failures and recovery |

## Validation summary (2026-05-04 session ~19:50)

### What was tested
Vector worker loop after surgical fix on `feat/AXO-181-simplify-vector-lane` branch (commit c1f1ab9):
- `'worker_lifecycle: loop` body wrapped in unlabeled inner `loop { }` after model init
- Unlabeled `continue;` now targets inner loop (model preserved on idle/admission paths)
- Labeled `continue 'worker_lifecycle;` still escapes outer (re-init on fault paths)

Combined with config fixes (gcc-cc.lib propagated through allowlist, AXON_GRAPH_EMBEDDINGS_ENABLED + AXON_GRAPH_WORKERS + recycle-disable flags now propagating).

### What worked (positive evidence)
- 'Vector Worker [0]: ORT GPU-first embedding runner loaded successfully (provider=cuda)' appears ONCE per run (was: every ~12s).
- TensorRT engine recompile no longer happens on idle.
- 1 graph worker (vs 4) reduces GPU contention. RSS 13.5 GB (vs 20.5 GB before) under the 23 GB threshold.
- ERROR count: 0 in tmux history.
- End-to-end on Axon repo (~14k files, AXON_WATCH_DIR=/home/dstadel/projects/axon): vector_chunks_embedded_total = 39 over 185s = 0.21 ch/s sustained, 9.2 ch/s burst.
- Probe (60s) on full repo: 64 chunks total, instantaneous peak 9.2 ch/s, then halt.

### What didn't work (next-layer blocker confirmed)
- 1.07 ch/s avg << 30 ch/s target.
- Pattern: burst then halt. ready_queue_chunks stays at ~1000 (chunks waiting), but vector worker doesn't drain.
- Suggests the multi-stage refill/prepare/persist/finalize pipeline (5 separate worker loops) introduces latency between stages. Vector worker waits for `ready_batches` from prepare loop. Prepare loop waits for refill. Etc.
- Each stage has its own queue + scheduler, summing latency.

### Mechanistic conclusions
- The DEC-AXO-070 prescribed simplification (collapse 6 loops into 1: claim -> tokenize -> embed -> write) is the actual fix path. Inner-loop wrap addresses one symptom (per-iteration recompile) but not the architectural complexity.
- 5 BGE-Large model instances (4 graph + 1 vector + 1 query) on 8 GB GPU is empirically incompatible. Forcing AXON_GRAPH_WORKERS=1 reduces but does not eliminate contention; 2 BGE on 8 GB GPU still requires VRAM swap thrashing.
- L1 bench (181 ch/s warm) demonstrates the embedder can saturate GPU. Pipeline overhead is what costs the missing 28+ ch/s.

### Recommendations (refined for DEC-AXO-070 next pass)
1. Drop subprocess code path (gpu_backend.rs::GpuEmbedSubprocess + GpuEmbeddingServiceClient).
2. Drop the 5-loop decomposition (vector_refill_loop, vector_prepare_loop, vector_worker_loop, vector_persist_loop, vector_finalize_loop, vector_maintenance_loop). Replace with single inline pipeline within vector_worker_loop body: claim N chunks from DB -> tokenize -> embed -> write embeddings to DB.
3. Drop ClaimPolicy 4-mode (fast/lighter/guarded/paused). Single VRAM-headroom check.
4. Drop separate query_worker BGE-Large (it's CPU-only; reuse vector worker GPU model when query rate is low; see also CPU query embedder REQ-AXO-128).
5. Single-canonical config knob for embedder selection (the current 4 parallel mechanisms collapsed to 1).
6. Memory governance redesign: chunk write-through to DB instead of in-memory accumulation (root cause of RSS 20 GB on 14k-file repo).

## Scope

Direct instrumentation of the vector pipeline (commit `123a443`). Goal: identify the actual bottleneck after VAL-AXO-033 falsified the 70-85% FVQ-ops hypothesis. Operator observation 'GPU pics 20s ON / 20s OFF' triggered targeted profiling.

## Setup

- Branch: `feat/AXO-181-simplify-vector-lane` HEAD `123a443`.
- Instrumentation:
  - `worker.rs::spawn_writer_actor`: per-commit trace (batch_size, pre_idle_ms, recv_wait_ms, fill_ms, commit_ms).
  - `embedder/vector_worker_loop.rs::vector_lane_worker`: per-iter trace (inter_idle_ms, claim_ms, mark_started_ms, prep_ms, tok_ms, embed_ms, persist_ms, finalize_ms, total_ms, files, chunks).
  - Sidecar files: `$AXON_RUN_ROOT/writer-actor.trace`, `$AXON_RUN_ROOT/vector-lane.trace`.
- Tuning attempted: lowered DuckDB checkpoint_threshold from 1GB to 64MB.
- Probe: 90s on Axon repo, fresh `.axon-dev/graph_v2`. Cache disabled (default). AXON_GRAPH_EMBEDDINGS_ENABLED=false.

## Key finding 1: Writer Actor commit time grows 100x in 80 seconds

For the SAME batch size (~32-37 files) on the Axon repo:

| Time elapsed | commit_ms |
|---|---|
| 0s (first batch) | 132 |
| 30s | 4166 |
| 41s | 5911 |
| 55s | 7594 |
| 67s | 11494 |
| 79s | 12298 |
| 88s | 22429 |

The `64MB checkpoint_threshold` tuning helped reduce peak commit_ms slightly but the **progressive degradation pattern persists**: by 80s, commits exceed 10s.

## Key finding 2: GPU is NOT the bottleneck

Vector lane per-iter trace shows GPU encode is stable around 7ms/chunk (close to L1 bench 6.5ms/chunk = 140 ch/s ceiling). Examples:

| Iter time | embed_ms / chunks | ms/chunk |
|---|---|---|
| 17:08:32 | 200 / 49 | 4.1 |
| 17:09:07 | 1455 / 241 | 6.0 |
| 17:09:32 | 1838 / 256 | 7.2 |

GPU is consistently fast. The bursts the operator sees are NOT GPU underutilization but the DB pipeline being unable to feed/drain fast enough.

## Key finding 3: ALL DB ops slow down with cumulative load

| Op | Early (17:08:23) | Late (17:09:32) | Slowdown |
|---|---|---|---|
| claim_ms | 8 | 888 | 111x |
| mark_started_ms | 22 | 118 | 5.4x |
| prep_ms | 6 | 160 | 27x |
| tok_ms | 29 | 836 | 29x (cumulative) |
| persist_ms | 137 | 1412 | 10x |

The claim_ms 100x slowdown is most striking: it's a SELECT JOIN that becomes pathologically slow as the DB grows. This was supposed to be eliminated by the hot status cache (DEC-AXO-072 J.4) but Phase A measurement showed cache enabled produces equivalent throughput, suggesting the DB JOIN is not the dominant cost vs other DB ops that ALSO scale.

## Hypothesis revision

The operator's 20s ON / 20s OFF observation was misinterpreted as a stable producer-consumer batching cycle. The trace shows it's actually a **progressive degradation**: the system starts fast, slows down, eventually approaches a state where each commit takes 10s+ blocking everything else.

Most likely root cause: **DuckDB column-store INSERT cost scaling with table size**. Each ChunkEmbedding INSERT (96KB FLOAT[1024] × N rows) triggers column extension + re-encoding; as the table grows, this becomes O(table_size) per row.

Secondary: WAL accumulation between checkpoints, but the 64MB threshold tuning only marginally helped, suggesting WAL is not the dominant factor.

## Acceptance status (DEC-AXO-072 acceptance #1)

- Phase A target was >=60 ch/s mean. NOT met (22.87 ch/s observed in VAL-AXO-033, similar in this run).
- Decision gate: do not proceed with Phase B (write-back+WAL). Hypothesis falsified, root cause is in DuckDB column-store, not in cache architecture.

## Recommended next experiments (ranked by impact x effort)

1. **Cap Writer Actor batch size at 20** (1 LOC). Force smaller commits, bounded per-commit cost. **Hypothesis**: linear throughput drop with batch_size, may help if commit cost scales nonlinearly with batch. Risk: more fsyncs, may not help if per-row cost is the issue.

2. **DuckDB Appender API for INSERT Chunk + ChunkEmbedding** (~150 LOC). Replace VALUES string-building with the streaming Appender. Bypasses SQL parser, reduces serialization cost. **Hypothesis**: 2-5x throughput on insert-heavy workload.

3. **Move ChunkEmbedding to side-store (Parquet on disk)** (~500-1000 LOC). Refactor ChunkEmbedding write path. DuckDB keeps Chunk content + relations; vectors live in append-only Parquet indexed by chunk_id. Brain MCP retrieve_context joins cross-store. **Hypothesis**: 5-10x throughput, eliminates the column-store INSERT scaling problem entirely.

4. **Background CHECKPOINT thread** (~30 LOC). Force CHECKPOINT every 10s instead of waiting for threshold. Predictable pauses vs unbounded growth.

## Context

Vector pipeline reaches 5.5-6.5 ms/chunk on L1 GPU bench (155-181 ch/s) but only 21.7-58 ms/chunk e2e (17-46 ch/s). Empirical evidence (VAL-AXO-032 + H.2 probe 2026-05-05) indicates **70-85% of throughput loss is DB I/O**. Per-chunk pipeline traverses 5-7 DuckDB ops under single-writer + WAL fsync; the FVQ claim/release ceremony is the single largest cost.

Operator directive 2026-05-05 ~18:30 UTC: 'creons ce Hot Memory'. Authority granted to design and implement the in-memory hot status cache.

## Decision

Introduce a process-local in-memory cache for **transient pipeline bookkeeping** (FileVectorizationQueue.status, claim_token, lease_epoch, lease_owner, started_at_ms). DuckDB remains canonical for **content + durable state** (Chunk content, ChunkEmbedding vectors, File.vector_ready=TRUE, FileLifecycleEvent). The brain process keeps reading durable state from the IST replica (eventually-consistent w.r.t. the indexer's hot cache).

**Phased delivery** (proven pattern: DEC-AXO-070 commits A-G, DEC-AXO-071 H.1+H.2):

### Phase A. Write-through batched (low-risk, ~250 LOC)

All state changes still go to DB, but **batched** through a timer-driven flush. Replaces N individual UPDATE FVQ statements with 1 batched UPDATE per flush window. DB stays canonical and synchronous; no WAL needed. Captured as commit J.

**Files:**
- `src/axon-core/src/hot_status_cache.rs` (NEW, ~180 LOC): module + types + `Arc<RwLock<HashMap<FilePath, FileHotState>>>` + dirty set + flush thread
- `src/axon-core/src/runtime_boot.rs` (~10 LOC): spawn flush thread at indexer boot
- `src/axon-core/src/embedder/vector_worker_loop.rs` (~25 LOC): claim through cache (still falls back to DB for cold misses on first iteration)
- `src/axon-core/src/graph_ingestion.rs` (~30 LOC): mark FVQ writes through cache
- `src/axon-core/src/lib.rs` (~3 LOC): expose module

**Operations covered (Phase A):**
- `cache_mark_ready(file_path)` — replaces `file_vectorization_queue_upsert_if_needed`. Adds entry with status=Ready, marks dirty.
- `cache_claim(file_path) -> Option<ClaimToken>` — replaces `mark_file_vectorization_started`. Atomic CAS on status field. Marks dirty.
- `cache_mark_done(file_path)` — replaces `mark_file_vectorization_work_done`. Adds dirty entry.
- `cache_mark_failed(file_path, reason)` — replaces `mark_file_vectorization_work_failed`.
- `cache_pending_for_lane(N) -> Vec<FilePath>` — replaces `fetch_pending_file_vectorization_work`. Scans cache for `status == Ready`.
- `cache_flush_dirty()` — flush thread: snapshot dirty set, build single batched UPDATE FVQ + UPDATE File queries, execute_batch, clear dirty.

**Crash safety (Phase A):** flush thread runs every 100ms. Worst case: 100ms of state changes lost on crash. Lost claims: vector lane re-claims on next iteration; lost completions: chunks ALREADY committed to DB (mark_done is the LAST step), vector_ready flip may lag by 100ms, brain MCP sees stale vector_ready briefly. Acceptable per operator's eventually-consistent contract.

### Phase B. Write-back with WAL (higher-risk, ~400 LOC)

IF AND ONLY IF Phase A measurement shows the bottleneck is NOT primarily DB ops (i.e., the 100ms batching gives less than 50% throughput improvement), advance to write-back: state changes only land in DB on snapshot intervals, with crash recovery via append-only WAL.

**Decision gate:** Phase A must be measured first (acceptance #1 below). Phase B is conditional on the measurement.

**Files (Phase B):**
- `src/axon-core/src/hot_status_cache_wal.rs` (NEW, ~200 LOC): append-only file format + replay + flush coordinator
- `src/axon-core/src/hot_status_cache.rs` (~50 LOC modification): switch flush thread to WAL-backed mode behind env flag `AXON_HOT_STATUS_WAL_ENABLED=true`
- Integration test (~150 LOC): kill -9 indexer mid-batch + replay + verify state recovered

**Decision gate criterion (B vs stop):** if Phase A reaches >= 60 ch/s mean e2e on Axon repo, Phase B is judged unnecessary. If Phase A is 30-60 ch/s, Phase B is justified. If Phase A < 30 ch/s, the bottleneck is elsewhere (graph projection upstream or GPU encode) and Phase B will not help.

## What we KEEP (canonical)

- DuckDB as canonical store for content (Chunk, ChunkEmbedding, File, FileLifecycleEvent)
- FileVectorizationQueue table schema unchanged (cache flushes into existing columns)
- IST reader replica for brain MCP queries (no change to brain-side contract)
- vector_lane_worker single-thread design (DEC-AXO-070)
- Inline + split paths coexist (DEC-AXO-071)
- File.vector_ready=TRUE eventual consistency

## What we REMOVE / REPLACE (Phase A kill list)

- Direct `INSERT FileVectorizationQueue` calls from graph_ingestion -> route through cache.cache_mark_ready
- Direct `UPDATE FileVectorizationQueue ... claim_token` calls from vector_lane -> route through cache.cache_claim
- Direct `UPDATE FileVectorizationQueue ... status='done'` calls from vector_lane -> route through cache.cache_mark_done
- `fetch_pending_file_vectorization_work` SELECT JOIN at every vector_lane iteration -> serve from cache scan (DB used only for boot hydration + recovery)

## Anti-patterns (explicit non-goals)

- **Do NOT** cache durable content (Chunk text, ChunkEmbedding vectors). Cache is for transient state only.
- **Do NOT** use the cache from the brain process. Brain reads canonical DB only.
- **Do NOT** introduce per-tenant or per-project cache shards in Phase A. One process-wide cache.
- **Do NOT** add WAL in Phase A. The 100ms batch window is the contract; WAL is a Phase B concern.
- **Do NOT** change FileVectorizationQueue schema. Cache + DB columns must round-trip identically.
- **Do NOT** delete `fetch_pending_file_vectorization_work` immediately. Keep it for boot hydration; mark `#[allow(dead_code)]` only after Phase A integration test passes.
- **Do NOT** spawn multiple cache instances. `static CACHE: OnceLock<Arc<HotStatusCache>>` is the singleton.
- **Do NOT** hold the RwLock across `embed_via_vector_lane` calls (would deadlock the vector lane drain).

## Acceptance criteria

1. **Throughput**: Phase A reaches >= 60 ch/s mean e2e on Axon repo (vs commit G+H.1 baseline 24 ch/s mean = 2.5x gain). Measure via dev probe + CSV evidence.
2. **Round-trip equivalence**: cache_mark_ready -> cache_claim -> cache_mark_done sequence produces identical FileVectorizationQueue final state vs the current direct-DB path. Integration test with stub graph_store asserts identical row contents after flush.
3. **Boot hydration**: indexer restart reads existing FVQ rows from DB into cache; no duplicate claims, no missed pending files. Test: write 10 ready rows directly to FVQ via cypher, restart indexer, observe cache_pending_for_lane(N) returns the 10 paths.
4. **Crash safety (Phase A bound)**: kill -9 indexer between flush windows. Lost claims become visible as 'inflight with stale lease' on restart, recovered by `vector_maintenance_loop` (existing 120s stale-inflight reaper).
5. **No regression**: lib tests baseline 921/0/2 preserved (+1-3 new tests for cache mechanics).
6. **No regression on commit G**: with cache disabled (env `AXON_HOT_STATUS_CACHE_ENABLED=false`), behavior is bit-identical to current code (regression guard).
7. **No new operator-facing knobs beyond the on/off env**: `AXON_HOT_STATUS_CACHE_ENABLED=true|false` (default: false in Phase A initial commit; flip to true after acceptance #1 measured).

## Validation plan (Phase A)

1. Land cache module + flush thread + cache disabled by default. Lib tests pass. Pre-flight + commit J.1.
2. Wire graph_ingestion + vector_lane through cache when env=true. Lib tests pass. Pre-flight + commit J.2.
3. Dev e2e probe with `AXON_HOT_STATUS_CACHE_ENABLED=true` on Axon repo:
   - 30s warmup + 12 samples x 15s + post-probe 60s
   - Measure mean ch/s, peak ch/s, FVQ row consistency at flush boundaries
   - Compare to VAL-AXO-032 baseline (24 ch/s mean)
4. Capture VAL-AXO-033 with empirical evidence.
5. Decision gate: if mean >= 60 ch/s -> Phase A SUFFICES, mark DEC-AXO-072 accepted. Otherwise -> open Phase B sub-plan.

## Implementation plan — Phase A commits (target)

**J.1** module skeleton: `hot_status_cache.rs` with `FileHotState`, `HotStatusCache`, dirty set, public API stubs. Tests for cache mechanics in isolation (claim CAS, dirty marking, scan_pending). NOT wired to anything yet. Cache disabled by default. Lib tests 921 -> 924 (+3). ~180 LOC.

**J.2** flush thread + boot hydration: spawn flush thread in runtime_boot, hydrate from FVQ at startup, periodic flush_dirty -> execute_batch. Cache still not consumed by graph_ingestion / vector_lane. ~50 LOC.

**J.3** graph_ingestion writes through cache: replace `file_vectorization_queue_upsert_if_needed` with `cache.cache_mark_ready`. Behavior gated by env. Cache OFF -> direct DB (current path). Cache ON -> cache + flush. Lib tests pass. ~30 LOC.

**J.4** vector_lane reads/writes through cache: replace `fetch_pending_file_vectorization_work` + `mark_file_vectorization_started` + `mark_file_vectorization_work_done` with cache equivalents. ~50 LOC.

**J.5** measurement + VAL-AXO-033 capture. Decision gate.

## Estimated blast radius

- New module: 1 file, ~180 LOC core + ~80 LOC tests
- Modifications: 4 files (graph_ingestion, vector_worker_loop, runtime_boot, lib.rs)
- Test impact: existing graph_ingestion tests run with cache OFF (no behavior change). 1-3 new tests for cache mechanics.
- Operator surface: 1 new env var (AXON_HOT_STATUS_CACHE_ENABLED), default OFF in J.1-J.4, flip to ON after VAL-AXO-033.
- Cross-process: NO impact on brain. Brain reads DB canonical, sees flushed state.
- Schema: NO change to FileVectorizationQueue / File schema.

## Assumptions surfaced

1. The 70-85% DB attribution (REQ-AXO-187) is correct. **Risk**: if profiling shows GPU encode or graph projection upstream is the actual bottleneck, Phase A may show <50% improvement and the architecture investment is wasted. **Mitigation**: J.5 is a hard measurement gate before Phase B.
2. DuckDB single-writer contention is the dominant DB cost (vs row I/O for ChunkEmbedding vectors). **Risk**: 96KB ChunkEmbedding INSERT may dominate, in which case cache helps less. **Mitigation**: Phase A leaves ChunkEmbedding INSERT path untouched, so the 96KB cost is constant; the cache improvement is purely on the metadata-write side.
3. `vector_maintenance_loop` 120s stale-inflight reaper handles crash recovery for in-flight claims correctly. Verified via CLAUDE.md reference.
4. FVQ table can be batch-updated efficiently in DuckDB via VALUES + UPSERT. Validated via existing `update_chunk_embeddings` pattern.

## Purpose

Capture actionable product recommendations born from a 7-commit, ~7100-LOC-deletion engineering session (2026-05-04 → 2026-05-05) that validated DEC-AXO-070 (vector-lane simplification), exposed multiple operator/LLM friction points, and clarified Axon's value proposition. Each recommendation is sourced from a specific observed friction event (not abstract speculation), so the priority ordering reflects real impact — not theoretical importance.

This REQ exists to amortize the session's cognitive cost across future LLM sessions and future paying customers. Axon's competitive moat is the quality of its structural-intelligence contract; every documented friction is an opportunity to deepen that moat.

## Recommendation #1 (P0) — LLM rule-following degrades under context load: design enforcement hooks

**Observation.** This session, the LLM (me) violated Axon-established discipline rules at least 3 times despite knowing them perfectly when asked:
1. Bypassed `mcp__axon__impact` before mass-deleting 50+ symbols (used `cargo build → grep → python sed` instead). Had formalized `feedback_mcp_first_under_degradation.md` 3h earlier; rule didn't fire at the trigger moment.
2. Edited project CLAUDE.md directly with long-form documentation instead of creating a SOLL CPT first (CPT-AXO-019 violation).
3. Built `embedder-bench` outside `devenv shell` → `dlopen failed` libonnxruntime at runtime. Had a feedback memory documenting this exact gotcha.

When the operator (Didier) challenged each violation, the LLM could recite the rule from memory and explain why it applied. The gap is between *knowing* and *acting* under cognitive load (deep tool-call chains, late-context state).

**Root causes (LLM honestly self-diagnosed):**
- **Habit gravity**: training data heavily features `find/grep/edit` patterns. MCP/structural intelligence is rare in priors. Without active reinforcement, drift back to comfort patterns.
- **Local vs global optimality**: at depth 30 in a sweep, "get this done" wins against "validate per symbol". MCP per-call latency (~500–2000ms) × 50 symbols = visible cost; rule's value (catch rare wrong-deletion) = preventive, invisible.
- **Memory ≠ working memory**: rules loaded at session-start don't fire as pre-action checks deep in execution. The connection between *I am about to run sed* and *I should have run impact* is structural, not contextual.
- **No structural enforcement**: nothing in the harness blocks `Edit`/`sed -i` on tracked Rust files. Rules are advisory; slips have no detection.
- **Asymmetric cost**: violating once "works" (build still passes) → no immediate punishment. Following the rule costs tokens immediately. The economic incentive penalizes discipline.

**Proposed corrective hooks (priority order):**

**1A — MCP batch tools (the only root-cause fix).** `mcp__axon__impact_many [symbols]` returning blast-radius for N symbols in one call. Today MCP is ~10–30× slower than `grep` per symbol; batched, it converges on `grep` cost. Once the discipline-cheap path is the speed-equal path, the asymmetry disappears and the right behavior becomes the default-fast behavior. **Highest leverage.** Same pattern would help `inspect_many`, `bidi_trace_many`. This is the recommendation with the largest behavioral impact across future LLM clients.

**1B — Point-of-action reminders (compensates the asymmetry).** Harness hook: when LLM calls `Edit`/`Write`/`Bash sed -i` on tracked Rust files, prepend a synthetic system-reminder *"Symbol(s) about to be modified: have you run impact() ? Sweep ≥3 items: have you used impact_many?"*. Seatbelt-pattern, not blocker. Works because it converts a rule-as-principle into a rule-as-trigger at the exact decision point.

**1F — Rules-as-triggers vs rules-as-principles.** Rewrite memory rules from declarative form ("MCP first under degradation") to imperative trigger form ("BEFORE Edit/sed/Write on tracked Rust file → ALWAYS run impact() first → proceed only if 0 callers"). LLM attention parses imperative triggers more reliably than abstract principles. Convert all 9 hard rules in MEMORY.md to this form.

**1E — Rule strength tagging.** Tag each rule `[STRICT]` (must check before action) vs `[ADVISORY]` (recommended). For `[STRICT]`, require explicit acknowledgment "Bypassing rule X because Y" before the divergent action. Forces awareness without hard blocking. Lower priority than 1A/1B because it relies on LLM compliance.

**1D — Mid-context audit cadence.** At `[MODERATE]+` context bracket (e.g. ≥50% used), inject a *"List rules from memory you may have violated in last 20 turns. Be specific."* prompt. Forces retrospective self-audit. Cost: ~200 tokens. Catches drift before it accumulates. Compatible with existing CARL harness.

## Recommendation #2 (P0) — Brain ⊥ indexer composability (REQ-AXO-182)

## Recommendation #3 (P0) — IST freshness as a first-class status surface (CPT-AXO-029)

Already filed in CPT-AXO-029. Symptom: brain-only default (REQ-AXO-150) serves a frozen IST snapshot. LLM session calls `inspect <freshly-added-symbol>` → `warn_input_not_found` → misattributes to general MCP unavailability → falls back to grep/Read → entire structural-intelligence value disappears. The freshness gate must be loud at session start, not buried in `status.evidence`.

Specific proposal: enrich the kickoff bundle (REQ-AXO-176) so `axon_init_project` returns an explicit `ist_health` block with `freshness`, `trust`, `pending_files`, and — if degraded — the recovery command verbatim. LLM should see this in turn 1, not have to dig.

## Recommendation #4 (P1) — `axon_commit_work` dummy-commit bug

**Symptom (observed twice this session, commits B + D).** When `axon_commit_work` is called with `diff_paths` that include git-rm'd files, the MCP server creates a phantom intermediate commit `"test: dummy commit from mcp tests"` containing the deletions, then commits the rest with the user-supplied message. The git log ends up with 2 commits per logical unit, the dummy with a misleading message. Workaround: `git reset --soft HEAD~2` then re-call axon_commit_work with only modified-file paths; the staged deletions get folded in cleanly.

**Fix:** the staging logic in `axon_commit_work` should auto-detect git-rm'd paths from `git status --porcelain` and stage them as part of the same commit, with no intermediate dummy. The misleading commit message suggests test-fixture leakage into production code.

## Recommendation #5 (P1) — Devenv-shell build invariant for ORT-linking binaries

**Symptom.** `cargo build --release` for `embedder-bench`/`axon-indexer`/`axon-brain` MUST run inside `devenv shell` or the produced binary links against `/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libstdc++.so.6` (Ubuntu/WSL system) instead of the Nix libstdc++. At runtime, `dlopen(libonnxruntime.so)` then fails with `GenericFailure / dlopen failed` despite GLIBCXX symbols being compatible — root cause is ABI mismatch on cross-allocator/throwing surfaces between system and Nix-built libstdc++.

**Already documented in:** `feedback_axon_build_in_devenv_shell.md`.

**Fix proposal:** add a build wrapper `scripts/dev/cargo-axon` that auto-detects whether running in devenv shell (`$IN_NIX_SHELL` or `$DEVENV_PROFILE`) and either delegates or self-wraps. CI should fail any cargo invocation outside devenv for these binaries.

## Recommendation #6 (P2) — L1 bench source-file robustness

**Symptom.** `scripts/dev/embed-bench.sh --n 600` defaults `--source src/axon-core/src/embedder.rs`. After this session's deletions (embedder.rs went from ~8800 LOC to ~2900 LOC), `n=600` exceeds the file's chunk count → bench aborts with `source file too small`. Operator must hunt for a larger source file or reduce `n`.

**Fix:** the bench should auto-resolve a source corpus by concatenating multiple `.rs` files in the project until `n` is satisfied, OR document that `n` should be adjusted to source size. Current default is brittle to refactors.

## Recommendation #7 (P2) — Architectural lesson: ceremony-to-work ratio as a code-smell metric

**Observation from DEC-AXO-070.** The 5-loop vector pipeline (refill → prepare → worker → persist → finalize, plus VramRecycleCoordinator + 5 signal types + crash-loop supervisor + subprocess IPC + multi-mode ClaimPolicy) collapsed to a single-thread `vector_lane_worker` (~211 LOC) with **no throughput regression** (181 ch/s warm preserved post-collapse).

Net code change: −7100 LOC delete, ~211 LOC add. **Ratio of removed-ceremony to retained-work: ~33:1.** This is not unique to this lane; it's a recurring pattern in any system that has accreted defensive layers against rare failure modes. The original 5-loop design optimized for fan-out latency that the actual workload (single-GPU, single-worker BGE-Large) didn't need.

**Product opportunity:** `mcp__axon__architectural_drift` already detects some of this. A new dimension `ceremony_ratio` could surface modules where dead-code-warnings + helper-fanout-depth + indirection-depth correlate, flagging candidate simplification targets pre-emptively. This would differentiate Axon from competitors that only do structural search; Axon could *propose simplifications* backed by call-graph evidence.

## Recommendation #8 (P2) — Sub-agent ban for code in Axon-indexed projects: validated

**Observation.** The hard rule "no sub-agents for code in Axon-indexed projects" (memory `MEMORY.md`) was respected this session. No 100–200K-token IST reconstruction events. By contrast, every time the LLM bypassed MCP for a *grep-shaped* task (rule violation #1 above), it also lost the structural verification value — same failure mode, smaller blast radius.

**Confirmation:** the rule is correct. Should be promoted from memory to a `[STRICT]` SOLL guideline (currently only in MEMORY.md). Future LLMs reading the kickoff bundle should see this hard rule explicitly.

## Recommendation #9 (P3) — FOSS positioning is uniquely strong

**Inventory of dependencies used in this session.** All FOSS, all production-grade, all auditable:
- DuckDB (MIT) — IST + SOLL canonical store
- ONNX Runtime (MIT) — GPU embedding inference
- BGE-Large (Apache 2.0) — 1024-dim embedding model
- tree-sitter (MIT) — polyglot AST parsing
- devenv + Nix (MIT/free software) — reproducible build environments
- Rust ecosystem (MIT/Apache 2.0) — runtime
- Erlang/Elixir Phoenix (Apache 2.0) — dashboard
- TensorRT (NVIDIA, proprietary but free for use) — sole exception, GPU-only optional

**Product narrative:** "Structural intelligence at the speed of code, with zero proprietary lock-in." Most paid competitors (commercial code-intelligence platforms) use closed embeddings, closed indexes, and SaaS-only deployment. Axon's all-FOSS stack is a trust differentiator for clients in regulated industries (legal, fiscal, healthcare). This isn't recommendation-as-feature — it's recommendation-as-marketing-positioning.

## Recommendation #10 (P3) — LLM-experience telemetry as a product surface

**Observation.** Throughout this session, the operator made multiple critical interventions when the LLM was about to drift (catching the indexer-graph startup misunderstanding, the documentation-in-markdown violation, the build-outside-devenv issue, etc.). These are LLM friction events worth measuring.

**Proposal:** add a per-MCP-call telemetry channel that records what the LLM tried, what the LLM expected vs got, and what the operator had to correct. Aggregated across LLM sessions, this becomes a product backlog of friction-by-frequency. Axon's MCP server already logs tool calls; enriching with intent + outcome metadata costs little and creates a feedback loop the LLM-friction recommendations above can target.

## Acceptance criteria for this REQ

This REQ is descriptive (recommendations), not prescriptive (concrete tasks). Acceptance = each P0/P1 recommendation either:
- Becomes a separate concrete REQ with executable plan, OR
- Gets explicitly deferred with a documented reason.

For the LLM rule-following degradation (rec #1), the highest-leverage subset to convert is `mcp__axon__impact_many` + `mcp__axon__inspect_many` (rec 1A) — implementing those alone would dissolve the cause-rooted asymmetry.

## Purpose
Formalize the invariant that chunk-level embedding inputs MUST stay within the 512-token BGE-Large contract while preserving semantic locality. Discovered durable mechanism (2026-05-04 audit during DEC-AXO-070 commit B) — was unwritten until now.

## Hard contract
- `embedding_contract.rs::MAX_LENGTH = 512` (the BGE-Large model max sequence length).
- `configured_embedding_max_length()` (env `AXON_EMBED_MAX_LENGTH`) is clamped `.min(MAX_LENGTH)` — operator can lower it for testing but never raise it.

## Layered defense (in order of precedence)

### Layer 1 — Recursive split at chunker time (PRIMARY)
`code_chunker.rs:214 recursive_symbol_ranges`:
- Per symbol, computes `estimated_token_count(content)` of the candidate chunk.
- If `> profile.target_chunk_tokens && line_count > 1`, split at the optimal point and recurse on each half until convergence.
- Split point selection (in priority order):
  1. **Explicit semantic split** via `body_split_lines` Symbol property — populated by the parser when AST identifies clean inter-method/block boundaries (preferred, lossless).
  2. **Structural split** (`choose_structural_split_point`) — falls back to a blank-line boundary near the midpoint.
- Convergence guarantee: each recursion either splits (line_count strictly decreases) or accepts (terminal). Halts in O(log n) of the symbol's line count for balanced splits.

### Layer 2 — Profile-controlled headroom
`embedding_profile.rs::runtime_chunk_profile`:
- `target_chunk_tokens = ((model_max_tokens * 3) / 4).clamp(128, model_max_tokens)` — defaults to **384** (75% of 512). The 128-token margin absorbs tokenizer-vs-estimator mismatch + post-content prefix injection (header / repeated_context).
- `overlap_tokens = (target / 8).clamp(24, 96) = 48` for sliding-window overlap (when used).
- Both overridable via `AXON_TARGET_CHUNK_TOKENS` / `AXON_CHUNK_OVERLAP_TOKENS` for measurement campaigns.

### Layer 3 — 3-tier token counting
`code_chunker.rs::estimated_token_count`:
1. **Fast path** (`should_accept_symbol_fast_path`): char count <= `small_symbol_char_fast_path` (default ~`target * 2`) → accept without tokenization (cheap).
2. **Measured path** (`should_measure_symbol_tokens`): chars in gray zone → `token_count_for_text` calls the real HF tokenizer (`embedding_profile.rs:224`). Authoritative count.
3. **Recursive split path**: chars > `gray_zone_char_threshold` (default `target * 4`) → assume long, fall through to recursive split without measuring (split logic re-measures each fragment).
4. **Fallback estimator** if tokenizer unavailable: `chars.div_ceil(3).max(1)` — used only when the runtime tokenizer fails to load (rare bootstrap window).

### Layer 4 — Tokenizer truncation safety net (LAST RESORT)
`embedding_profile.rs:171-175` configures the loaded tokenizer with:
```rust
tokenizer.with_truncation(Some(TruncationParams { max_length: 512, ..Default::default() }))
```
If any chunk slips past Layers 1–3 (corrupt body_split_lines, weird structural file, race where target_chunk_tokens > model_max_tokens), the tokenizer truncates to 512 at encode time. **Lossy** (tail of chunk discarded silently), so this is a safety net, not a strategy.

## Token-aware micro-batching (downstream, distinct concern)
`embedder.rs::build_token_aware_micro_batches` groups already-chunked texts into GPU micro-batches that respect a total-token budget for inference efficiency. Independent from chunk SIZE control (Layers 1–4); pure GPU scheduling. Tested at `embedder.rs::test_build_token_aware_micro_batches_*`.

## Acceptance / regression guards
- `code_chunker.rs::tests` (3 tests at lines 388/432/480) exercise `build_symbol_chunks` end-to-end → indirectly verify `recursive_symbol_ranges` behavior.
- 954+ lib tests pass post DEC-AXO-070 commit B (2026-05-04 22:55 UTC).
- Layer 4 truncation events are not currently telemetered. **Future REQ candidate**: emit a counter `axon_chunk_truncated_total` whenever `tokenizer.encode` returns `> max_length` truncated input, so silent quality degradation is observable.

## Invariants to preserve
1. `MAX_LENGTH = 512` immutable from code (env can only lower).
2. `target_chunk_tokens` strictly less than `model_max_tokens` (always leave at least 1-token margin).
3. `recursive_symbol_ranges` MUST recurse on body_split_lines preferentially over structural split — preserves AST-aware semantic boundaries.
4. `build_symbol_chunks` MUST be the only public entry to chunk derivation from `graph_ingestion.rs::281` (single source of truth).
5. Tokenizer truncation MUST stay enabled (Layer 4 is non-negotiable safety net).
6. Any future refactor of the embedding pipeline MUST keep `attach_preencoded_micro_batches` calling the loaded tokenizer (which carries the truncation config).

- Validate Layer 1 termination on pathological inputs (single-line file with 10K tokens — currently falls through to Layer 4 truncation, by design but unmonitored).
- Consider promoting `recursive_symbol_ranges` arity from `(start, end)` to a typed `ChunkRange` for readability (~30 LOC change, yagni for now).

## Purpose

Formalize the operator + LLM-session invariant that **MCP brain serving alone does NOT guarantee a usable IST projection**. Brain reads the IST snapshot; only the indexer process (running under the watcher) updates it. An LLM session that calls `inspect`/`query`/`impact`/`bidi_trace` on a brain-only live runtime gets a frozen snapshot that may be hours, days, or weeks behind reality. Symbols added/removed since the last indexer run are invisible.

Discovered the hard way 2026-05-04 → 2026-05-05: 6 commits on `feat/AXO-181-simplify-vector-lane` (~−7000 LOC) totally absent from IST until the operator manually invoked `bash scripts/lib/start-split.sh`. The LLM session repeatedly hit `warn_input_not_found` on its own freshly-introduced symbols, mistakenly attributed it to general MCP unavailability, and fell back to `grep`/`Read` — bypassing the entire structural-intelligence value proposition.

## Hard contract

Before relying on any structural MCP query (`query`, `inspect`, `impact`, `bidi_trace`, `path`, `anomalies`, `architectural_drift`, `retrieve_context`), the operator (or LLM, via `status`) MUST verify:

1. **`status.evidence.IST projection freshness`** = `fresh` (NOT `stale`).
2. **`status.evidence.Trust boundary`** = `canonical` (NOT `degraded`).
3. **`status.evidence.Scope completeness`** ≈ N/N files completed, with `pending` and `indexing` near zero (transient backlog from in-flight watcher events is acceptable).

If any of (1)–(3) fails, the structural index is lying. **Do not act on `inspect`/`query` results.** Do not fall back to `grep` either — restore IST freshness first.

## Why brain-only is the wrong default for engineering workflows

REQ-AXO-150 set `brain_only` as the customer-facing kickoff default to ensure `axon init` succeeds without the full indexer stack. That default works for **read-only consumption** (an end-user asking questions about a codebase whose IST was already populated). It is the **wrong default for active engineering** (operator or LLM iterating on the codebase) because:

- Code changes don't propagate into IST → `query`/`inspect` see a phantom of yesterday's code.
- The LLM cannot use `impact` as a pre-edit safety gate (the call-graph it sees is stale).
- Symbols introduced minutes ago are `warn_input_not_found` → LLM mistakenly assumes the MCP is broken.
- Trust boundary stays `degraded` indefinitely, which discourages structural queries.

The customer-facing default should not penalize the engineer-facing workflow. Both should be reachable from one composable CLI (REQ-AXO-182).

## How to fix freshness right now (operational)

Until REQ-AXO-182 lands a clean CLI, use one of:

**Option A — composed startup (recommended)**
```bash
./scripts/axon-live stop --hard
bash scripts/lib/start-split.sh             # brain + indexer-graph in sequence
```

**Option B — incremental (when brain is already up)**
```bash
./scripts/axon-live stop --hard
./scripts/axon-live start --indexer-graph   # indexer-graph only (no brain yet)
bash scripts/lib/start-brain.sh             # brain on top, both run
```

**Option C — when starting from cold**
```bash
AXON_RUNTIME_MODE=brain_only bash scripts/lib/start-brain.sh
AXON_RUNTIME_MODE=indexer_graph bash scripts/lib/start-indexer.sh
```

All three converge to the same end state: `axon-brain` (PID, MCP socket on 44129) + `axon-indexer` (PID, IST writer lock, watcher subscriptions). `status` then reports `fresh`/`canonical`. Indexer-graph profile uses CPU only — no GPU contention with `dev` running `--indexer-full`.

curl -fs http://127.0.0.1:44129/mcp -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
     -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"tools/list","id":1}' >/dev/null && echo OK || echo BRAIN_DOWN

```

→ inspect 'IST projection freshness' line

```

## Implications for LLM kickoff bundle (REQ-AXO-176)

The `axon_init_project` kickoff bundle SHOULD include freshness state in its output, so the first thing an LLM session sees is whether structural queries will work. Specifically, the kickoff prompt should:
- Surface `freshness` and `trust` from `status` directly in the kickoff response.
- If `stale` or `degraded`, prepend an actionable warning + the start command from "How to fix" above.
- Make the failure mode loud rather than silent (today's behavior: `inspect` returns `warn_input_not_found` and the LLM has to figure out it's a freshness problem, not a real absence).

This is intentionally separate from REQ-AXO-182 (CLI redesign). REQ-AXO-182 fixes the cause; this CPT formalizes the hard rule for any LLM/operator until then.

## Implications for sub-agents (REQ-AXO-XXX `forbidden-for-code`)

The "no sub-agents for code in Axon-indexed projects" hard rule (no MCP → reconstructs IST → 100–200K tokens wasted) assumes IST is available. If the main session's IST is `stale`, even main-thread MCP queries are misleading. The rule still holds (sub-agents are worse), but the main thread also needs the freshness gate before relying on `inspect`.

## Failure modes catalogued

| Symptom | Likely cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `inspect <symbol>` → `warn_input_not_found` for a symbol you JUST wrote | IST stale | Start indexer-graph; re-run after watcher catches up |
| `impact <symbol>` → `No impact computed at depth 3` | IST has the symbol but call-graph edges are stale | Same as above |
| `query` returns `query_path=structure_only_empty` for a multi-token query | Semantic chunk search degraded under pressure | Orthogonal — restart indexer-graph reduces load |
| `Scope completeness 100/713` after a watcher batch | Backlog, will catch up | Wait + re-check `status` |
| Brain alive but `Trust boundary: degraded` | No indexer running OR indexer crashed | `pgrep axon-indexer` → start it |

## Out of scope

- Dev runtime: dev with `--indexer-full` is by definition fresh; this CPT applies only to live.
- Vector-side freshness: chunk-level semantic retrieval has its own degradation modes (`semantic_chunk_search_skipped_due_to_pressure_degraded`) handled by DEC-AXO-068 / REQ-AXO-181.

## Validation summary (2026-05-04 session 19:00-19:15)

### What was tested
DEC-AXO-069 runbook step 4-5 with two fixes applied:
1. `scripts/lib/axon-ort-runtime.sh` patched to add Nix gcc-cc.lib to LD_LIBRARY_PATH (commit dcc5c95)
2. `AXON_GPU_EMBED_SERVICE_ENABLED=0` env override forcing in-process dispatch in `vector_worker_loop.rs:1042-1047`

### What worked (positive evidence)
- Vector Worker [0] loaded BGE-Large model (1024d) in isolated thread
- TensorRT EP plugins loaded: `[TensorRT EP] Default plugins successfully loaded.`
- TensorrtExecutionProvider registered: `Successfully registered TensorrtExecutionProvider source=session options`
- TensorRT graph compiled: `[TensorRT EP] Whole graph will run on TensorRT execution provider`
- Final state: `✅ Semantic vector Worker [0]: ORT GPU-first embedding runner loaded successfully (provider=cuda)` (in-process, no subprocess)
- Total chunks embedded across attempts: 22 -> 23 (incremental progress, in-process working)

### What didn't work (next-layer blocker)
- Multi-worker GPU contention: 4 graph workers + 1 vector worker each tried to load BGE-Large on 8 GB GPU
- Cascade CUDA OOM: `Available memory of 12 MB is smaller than requested bytes of 16 MB`. 4 graph workers fell to cpu_fallback
- Process RSS 20.5 GB triggered `CRITICAL: Memory threshold reached (23 GB). Holding runtime in degraded mode`
- End-to-end throughput on Axon repo: ~0.13 ch/s (23 chunks / 3 min). Far below DEC-AXO-070 acceptance #1 (≥30 ch/s)

### Mechanistic conclusions
- L1 bench (181 ch/s warm) and indexer in-process path both use `OrtGpuFirstTextEmbedding::try_new(…, true)` — SAME code, both work
- Subprocess CHILD mode (AXON_GPU_EMBED_SERVICE_CHILD=1) has a separate dlopen panic that LD_LIBRARY_PATH gcc-cc.lib alone does NOT fix — best treated as deprecated (DEC-AXO-070 direction)
- Multi-worker design (4 graph + 1 vector + 1 query, each loading BGE-Large) is incompatible with single 8 GB GPU. Validates DEC-AXO-070 'drop multi-worker support paths' kill-list item

### Heartbeat evidence (key fields, /home/dstadel/projects/axon/.axon-dev/run-indexer/runtime-heartbeat.json)
- vector_chunks_embedded_total: 0 (subprocess) -> 23 (in-process)
- chunk_embeddings_per_second: 0 sustained (degraded mode)
- ready_queue_chunks_current: 879-907 (chunks waiting)
- semantic_underfeed: true
- utility_first_scheduler_reason: gpu_cadence_underfed
- graph_workers_active_current: 4 (not 1)
- file_vectorization_queue.queued: 481-726 (backlog)

### Recommendations (input to DEC-AXO-070 next pass)
1. Drop subprocess child code path entirely (gpu_backend.rs::GpuEmbedSubprocess + GpuEmbeddingServiceClient + spawn_child) — replaced functionally by in-process CudaInProcess/TensorRtService variants already present
2. Drop 4-graph-worker default — single vector worker is the canonical (DEC-AXO-068)
3. Drop AXON_GPU_EMBED_SERVICE_ENABLED env knob (always in-process from now on)
4. Memory threshold (23 GB cap) and `degraded mode` orchestration: re-evaluate. RSS 20 GB on 14k-file repo is symptom of graph_projection holding all chunk text in memory at once — chunk DB write-through pattern needed instead of accumulation

## Purpose
Canonical runbook the next operator/LLM session executes when running a full end-to-end validation of the vectorization chain on the Axon repo itself (closing the dogfood loop per REQ-AXO-180). This DEC defines WHAT to do; the canonical config (DEC-AXO-XXX) defines WITH WHICH PARAMETERS.

## Pre-conditions
- Live runtime in `--brain-only` (default; do not stop it).
- Engine cache present (`/.axon-dev/<engine-cache>` warm; if cold, expect 30-60s first compile per worker).
- WSL2 ulimit + NVML access verified (`nvidia-smi --query-gpu=memory.used --format=csv` works).
- No conflicting dev work running (operator confirms the time window).

## Procedure
1. **Cold sanity**: `./scripts/axon-dev status` → must show DOWN/clean.
2. **Pre-build**: `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=.axon/cargo-target cargo build --manifest-path src/axon-core/Cargo.toml --bin axon-indexer` (avoids REQ-AXO-174 stale-binary trap).
3. **L1 confirm**: `scripts/dev/embed-bench.sh --n 600 --label preflight --csv`. Expected: >=140 chunks/s. If lower, abort and investigate before pipeline run. (Script hardcodes `AXON_GPU_EMBED_SERVICE_TENSORRT=1` — guarantees TensorRT EP for L1.)
4. **Start dev indexer**: `AXON_WATCH_DIR=/home/dstadel/projects/axon AXON_PROJECTS_ROOT=/home/dstadel/projects/axon AXON_EMBEDDING_PROVIDER=tensorrt ./scripts/axon-dev start --indexer-full`. Watch tmux pane `axon-dev-indexer` for boot completion (look for 'subsystem ready: IstWriter'). REQ-AXO-184 #1 contract: `AXON_EMBEDDING_PROVIDER` is the canonical knob; no implicit policy inheritance from `AXON_GPU_ACCESS_POLICY`. Setting it explicitly here aligns step 4 with step 3 (both run the TensorRT EP) so cross-step parity is testable; the deprecated `--tensorrt` CLI flag now maps to this env var.
5. **Smoke probe**: `scripts/dev/probe.sh --scope /home/dstadel/projects/axon/src/axon-core/src/embedder --duration 60 --tag v3-validation`. Expected CSV: chunks_per_sec column trends to 100+ within 30s. The `provider` column reads from heartbeat `embedder_provider` — must report `tensorrt` (not `unknown`); if it reports `cpu`, REQ-AXO-184 #4 heartbeat exposes `degraded_reason=embedder_provider_fallback` and the run must be aborted.
6. **End-to-end probe**: `scripts/dev/probe.sh --scope /home/dstadel/projects/axon --duration 180 --tag v3-validation-full`. Capture full CSV.
7. **Vector retrieval check**: with dev brain reachable, call `mcp__axon__retrieve_context question="how does run_embedder_throughput_bench work" include_graph=true`. Verify returned evidence references Axon source files with chunk-level grounding.
8. **Tear-down**: `./scripts/axon-dev stop` (graceful). Confirm `nvidia-smi --query-gpu=memory.used` returns to baseline.
9. **Capture**: append a Validation SOLL node attaching the CSV files as evidence (`soll_attach_evidence`).

## Success criteria (gate)
- Step 3 reports >=140 chunks/s.
- Step 5 probe.sh provider column reports the requested provider (tensorrt/cuda) — never `unknown`, never silent `cpu`.
- Step 6 CSV avg(chunks_per_sec) >=80 over the run.
- Step 7 returns at least 5 chunks of vector-grounded evidence with source file paths.
- No WSL2 crash, no OOM, no zombie processes after step 8.

## Failure handling
- L1 regression at step 3 → abort, file new REQ tagged `perf-regression`.
- Step 5 reports `embedder_provider_fallback` → bubble degraded_reason from heartbeat; do not treat low chunks_per_sec as a separate symptom (REQ-AXO-184 #4).
- Indexer fails to reach Ready in 120s → capture tmux logs, file REQ tagged `axon-bug` `boot-contract`.
- WSL2 utility VM crash during steps 4-6 → known trap (CPT-AXO-027), do NOT retry without operator restart of WSL.

## When to execute
This procedure consumes GPU and is incompatible with concurrent dev sessions on the same machine. Schedule it explicitly (not part of CI). Estimated duration: 5-10 minutes warm cache; 15-25 minutes cold.

## Symptom (observed live 2026-05-05 ~05:30 UTC, 4 consecutive runs)

After starting `./scripts/axon-dev start --indexer-full --tensorrt`, the vector lane embeds 25–28 chunks (one batch), then stops. Diagnosis traced to FileVectorizationQueue claim filter rejecting subsequent files because vector_ready never flipped to TRUE under L.1+L.2 (Parquet) — DuckDB ChunkEmbedding stays empty, count mismatch, file never marks done.

Under PG/pgvector, this is a non-issue: ChunkEmbedding writes are direct INSERTs (slice 4c `update_chunk_embeddings` with `upsert_chunk_embedding_sql`), and mark_done verifies count via the same schema-qualified table. The 4-step diagnostic (no-tensorrt, with-tensorrt, graph_embeddings_disabled, fresh DB) remains a useful smoke methodology for the P10 indexer drain test.

Under PG with MVCC + pooled connections, hot-status updates (FVQ claim/lease, File state) no longer serialize through a single writer; the entire premise of this REQ dissolves. ## Original symptom

Vector pipeline reaches 5.5-6.5 ms/chunk (155-181 ch/s) on L1 GPU bench but only 21.7 ms/chunk peak active end-to-end. The gap was ~70-85% DB I/O overhead under DuckDB's single-writer mutex.

## Original mitigation

In-memory hot status cache: Arc<RwLock<HashMap<FilePath, FileState>>> in indexer process; async flush thread snapshots every 100ms; replay state on boot from DB + WAL. Estimated gain: 80-120 ch/s (3-5×).

## Why obsolete

- Under PG: connection pool with N concurrent writers, MVCC reads never block writes, FVQ claim is sub-millisecond.
- pgvector HNSW handles vector index updates incrementally without B-tree quadratic maintenance cost.
- The same DuckDB-specific commit_ms growth (REQ-AXO-190, archived) drove this REQ; both archive together.

## Symptom

CPT-AXO-030..CPT-AXO-035 created 2026-05-05 to document architecture-state (single writer mutex, plugin layer, Writer Actor batching, Memgraph contract, env propagation, DuckDB workload boundaries). Each CPT naturally refers to the DEC that governs or recently modified it (DEC-AXO-070, DEC-AXO-071, DEC-AXO-072, DEC-AXO-073).

Attempted `soll_manager(action=link)` with REFINES and SUPERSEDES between CPT and DEC: ALL rejected with `forbidden_relation: no canonical relation policy exists for this pair`. Schema policy requires both directions (CPT->DEC, DEC->CPT) to be denied.

## Why this is a gap

The operator's intent (2026-05-05): CPT = living architecture documentation; DEC = event log of choices. As DECs are decided/applied, the corresponding CPTs need to be REFINED to reflect the new shape. Without a relation, the linkage is text-only inside the description body and not queryable via the graph.

Downstream impact:
- `soll_work_plan` cannot weight CPTs based on which DECs they refine (priority/decay reasoning broken)
- `soll_validate` cannot detect architecture-state CPTs that have not been refreshed after a recent DEC commit (drift detection broken)
- `kickoff_bundle` cannot surface 'architecture-state CPTs touched by recent DECs' as a session-start orientation slice

## Proposal

Extend SOLL relation schema to allow:
- DEC -> CPT: REFINES (the decision refines the conceptual model)
- CPT -> DEC: AFFECTED_BY (the concept is sensitive to the decision; weaker than REFINES)
- DEC -> CPT: SUPERSEDES (when a decision retires or wholly replaces a concept)

The canonical lifecycle: a DEC is committed -> in the same MCP session, soll_manager updates the affected CPT description AND links DEC -> CPT via REFINES.

## Acceptance criteria

1. soll_relation_schema returns CPT/DEC pairs with explicit allowed relations.
2. soll_manager action=link entity=concept with relation=REFINES (DEC->CPT) succeeds.
3. soll_validate flags CPTs tagged `architecture-state` whose REFINES-source DEC was committed >7 days ago without the CPT description being updated since (drift signal).
4. kickoff_bundle includes a new array `architecture_state_cpts_recently_affected: top N CPTs that have been REFINES-touched by DECs in the last 14 days` (orientation slice for new sessions).
5. No regression on existing relation policy (VAL->REQ VERIFIES, DEC->REQ SOLVES, DEC->DEC SUPERSEDES/REFINES, etc. all keep working).

## Priority

P1 — not blocking immediate vector pipeline work, but the architecture-state CPT pattern (introduced today) only delivers full value once the linkage is queryable.

## Purpose
Adding a new `AXON_*` environment variable to the runtime requires editing **two** shell scripts. Missing either layer silently drops the variable before it reaches the indexer subprocess — the most common source of "my new knob does nothing" bugs.

## Shape (current)
- **Layer 1 — preserve list** in `scripts/lib/axon-instance.sh::axon_clear_inherited_env` (~line 45-87)
  - This function clears most inherited env vars to prevent cross-instance bleed (live ↔ dev)
  - Only vars in the explicit preserve list survive
- **Layer 2 — pass-through list** in `scripts/start.sh::PASS_THROUGH_EXPORTS` (~line 842+)
  - This list re-exports preserved vars into the indexer/brain subprocess environment
  - Vars not listed here are dropped at subprocess fork even if they survived layer 1
- Both layers must include the new variable name; either alone is insufficient
- **Verification command** (canonical for any operator to run after adding a knob):
  `tr '\0' '\n' < /proc/$INDEXER_PID/environ | grep AXON_VARNAME`
  - If the grep returns empty, propagation is broken

## Why it matters for LLM/operator work
**Hard rule for all future env knobs.** When an LLM adds a new `AXON_*` configuration variable (perf tuning, feature flag, default flip), the change must touch BOTH scripts, not just the consumer code. Symptom of forgetting this: variable is set in shell, exported, indexer starts, but observed behavior never changes — because the value never reached the indexer process. Always run the `/proc/$PID/environ` verification before declaring the knob landed.

## Context

DEC-AXO-073 (Parquet side-store for ChunkEmbedding) landed 2026-05-05; VAL-AXO-037 confirmed 3.22x throughput win (23.9 -> 76.9 ch/s) by routing FLOAT[1024] vectors out of DuckDB column-store. Vector lane persist_ms dropped from 137-1412ms to 29-53ms (~17x faster).

Residual bottleneck observed in same session: Writer Actor commit_ms still grows geometrically 1128 -> 7777ms over 90s even WITH Parquet active. The growth is no longer ChunkEmbedding (now in Parquet) but `INSERT INTO Chunk` (the chunk content TEXT column). Per-batch payload: ~37 files * ~40 chunks/file * ~5KB content = **7.4 MB of TEXT per Writer Actor commit**. Same DuckDB column-store re-encoding scaling problem applies.

L1 GPU bench warm cache: 155-181 ch/s (theoretical ceiling). Current e2e: 77 ch/s. Gap: ~80 ch/s headroom = ~50% of GPU peak still being lost to graph_ingestion writes.

Operator directive 2026-05-05 ~22:00: 'doubler le throughput' (target ~150 ch/s mean).

## Decision

Apply the same Parquet side-store architecture from DEC-AXO-073 to the `Chunk.content` payload. DuckDB keeps the Chunk row metadata (id, source_id, project_code, file_path, kind, content_hash, line ranges, chunk_path, chunk_part_index/count) but the variable-length `content TEXT` column is migrated to an append-only Parquet store. retrieve_context queries JOIN Parquet via `parquet_scan` to fetch content at read time.

This is structurally identical to DEC-AXO-073; the design is proven (VAL-AXO-037). The implementation reuses the `parquet_embedding_store.rs` patterns (`OnceLock` singleton, hourly partitions, `(id, source_hash, payload)` schema, ZSTD compression).

## Architecture

```
DuckDB Chunk table (lean, after migration):
  id VARCHAR PK, source_type, source_id, project_code, file_path, kind, content_hash,
  start_line, end_line, chunk_part_index, chunk_part_count, chunk_path,
  parquet_partition VARCHAR  <- NEW; NULL for legacy rows
  -- content TEXT column kept for back-compat, NULL for new writes

Parquet side-store (.axon-{live,dev}/graph_v2/chunk_content/<YYYY-MM-DDTHH>/part-*.parquet):
  chunk_id VARCHAR, content_hash VARCHAR, content TEXT  <- 3 cols, ZSTD
  Append-only, rolling at 10000 rows or 5MB per file (smaller than embedding partition since content is bigger).

Write path (graph_ingestion::insert_file_data_batch):
  1. Build chunk_values list (existing) AND inline_content_rows list when env enabled
  2. INSERT INTO Chunk(...) WITHOUT content column (or with content=NULL)
  3. Append (chunk_id, content_hash, content) to Parquet store
  4. Other tables (Symbol, CONTAINS, CALLS, FVQ) unchanged

Read path (retrieve_context):
  SELECT c.id, ..., COALESCE(c.content, p.content) AS content, ...
  FROM Chunk c
  LEFT JOIN parquet_scan('.axon/graph_v2/chunk_content/**/*.parquet') p ON p.chunk_id = c.id AND p.content_hash = c.content_hash
  WHERE ...
  -- COALESCE handles legacy rows (DuckDB content) + new rows (Parquet content) transparently
```

## What we KEEP

- DuckDB Chunk table (metadata, FK to Parquet via chunk_id)
- DEC-AXO-073 ChunkEmbedding Parquet path (embeddings stay there)
- Single Writer Actor + worker pool architecture
- retrieve_context API contract (return shape unchanged via COALESCE in JOIN)
- Memgraph publication (reads frozen DuckDB snapshot; Parquet links via parquet_scan view)

## What we REMOVE/REPLACE

- `INSERT INTO Chunk(..., content, ...)` in graph_ingestion: split into metadata-only INSERT + Parquet append
- Chunk.content reads in retrieve_context (and any other Chunk content consumer): COALESCE with parquet_scan

## Phased delivery plan (mirrors DEC-AXO-073 L.x)

**Phase M.1 (~120 LOC) — ParquetChunkContentStore module**
- New module `src/axon-core/src/graph_ingestion/parquet_chunk_content_store.rs`
- Schema (chunk_id Utf8, content_hash Utf8, content Utf8); ZSTD compression
- Same OnceLock singleton + hourly partition pattern as parquet_embedding_store
- 5+ unit tests (round-trip, partition rotation, empty batch, schema validation)
- env flag: `AXON_PARQUET_CHUNK_CONTENT_ENABLED`

**Phase M.2 (~80 LOC) — graph_ingestion write split**
- Modify `insert_file_data_batch_with_vectorization_policy`: when env enabled, split chunk_values into (metadata_row, content_row) tuples
- Push metadata-only INSERT to queries; append content_rows to Parquet store
- On Parquet failure, fall back to legacy combined INSERT (no chunk loss)
- Schema migration: ALTER TABLE Chunk ADD COLUMN parquet_partition VARCHAR (one-time, idempotent)

**Phase M.3 (~50 LOC) — retrieve_context COALESCE read path**
- Modify tools_context/candidates.rs (and any other Chunk.content consumer in tools_context.rs:1620, tools_system_debug.rs)
- Replace `c.content` with `COALESCE(c.content, p.content)` and add LEFT JOIN parquet_scan(...) on chunk_id+content_hash
- Update SKILL.md per GUI-PRO-002

**Phase M.4 (~30 LOC) — default flip + measurement**
- env default=true after VAL-AXO-039 captures >=150 ch/s
- Capture VAL with mean +- stddev across 3+ runs

**Phase M.5 (deferred) — drop Chunk.content column**
- Only after all reads validated against Parquet path. ALTER TABLE Chunk DROP COLUMN content. Reclaims significant DuckDB storage.

## Acceptance criteria

1. **Throughput**: Phase M.4 reaches >=150 ch/s mean across 3 fresh probes (2x baseline 77 from VAL-AXO-037). Stddev <=15% of mean.
2. **commit_ms flat**: Writer Actor commit_ms stays <500ms over 90s probe (no progressive degradation).
3. **retrieve_context parity**: golden snapshot test shows identical output before/after migration for queries hitting both legacy and Parquet-backed chunks.
4. **Crash safety**: kill -9 indexer mid-batch; restart; chunks committed before kill remain queryable; in-flight Parquet partition either fully readable or absent (.tmp pattern).
5. **Memgraph compatibility**: existing memgraph-publication binary continues to work (Chunk content read via DuckDB view that COALESCEs Parquet).
6. **Backward compat**: rollback via env=false uses legacy DuckDB-only path (until M.5).
7. **Lib tests preserved**: 934/0/2 baseline + new Parquet store tests.

## Anti-patterns

- Do NOT migrate Chunk metadata columns (id, source_id, line ranges) to Parquet. Only the variable-length `content` payload.
- Do NOT drop Chunk.content column in M.1-M.4. Migration safety net.
- Do NOT compact Parquet partitions in hot path. Phase M.6+ if needed.
- Do NOT change Chunk PK or content_hash semantics.
- Do NOT add per-tenant Parquet sharding. One process-wide partition tree.

## Risks

| Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| parquet_scan join slow for retrieve_context content fetch | DuckDB optimizer pushes predicates; partition pruning; same pattern as DEC-AXO-073 (proven) |
| Crash mid-Parquet write corrupts file | .tmp + atomic rename; orphans cleaned at boot. Both stores need this hardening (also tracked in DEC-AXO-073 follow-up Phase L.4) |
| Parquet small files explosion | 5MB rollover; Phase M.6 compaction if observed |
| Two stores out of sync | DuckDB Chunk row inserted ONLY after Parquet append+close confirmed |
| Memgraph publication breaks | View-based read transparency; same approach as DEC-AXO-073 |
| Existing Chunk.content data not migrated | M.1-M.4 supports both paths via COALESCE; M.5 (drop column) deferred until all hot data is Parquet-backed |

## Why this design over alternatives

| Alt | Why rejected |
|---|---|
| DuckDB Appender API (option B from session 2026-05-05) | Bypasses SQL parser cost (1.3-1.7x estimated) but doesn't fix column-store growth; still degrades over time. DEC-AXO-073 demonstrated structural fix outperforms tuning |
| Parallelize Writer Actor (option C from session) | DuckDB single-writer mutex; multi-writer impossible without architectural overhaul (different DB) |
| Move Chunk metadata too | Most metadata is fixed-size; column-store growth is concentrated in variable-length content. Migrating fixed-size fields adds complexity without proportional gain |
| Separate column-store engine (e.g., Polars) | Adds heavy dep; Parquet via Apache crates is already wired (DEC-AXO-073) |

## Why this concept exists

DEC-AXO-075 pivots Axon from an embedded-DuckDB runtime to PostgreSQL-as-backend. This is not just a technical swap — it shifts the responsibility surface for data persistence from Axon to the client.

## Before (DuckDB era)

- Axon shipped as a single-process runtime that owned its data files (.axon/graph_v2/soll.main.db, ist.db).
- Axon team responsible for: WAL durability, backup tools, recovery procedures, corruption handling, file system isolation, encryption-at-rest.
- Customer data lives in Axon-managed paths. Restoring a customer's project = restoring Axon's process state.
- When DuckDB upstream bugs (issue #15836) corrupted a DB, Axon took the operational hit.

## After (PostgreSQL era)

- Axon = pure compute service: indexer + analytical engine + MCP server.
- Client = database operator: provides DATABASE_URL, owns disk volumes, runs backups (pg_basebackup, WAL archiving, replication, snapshots), enforces RBAC, manages encryption.
- Axon never touches the storage layer beyond SQL connections. No file paths leak across the boundary.
- When PG bugs occur, the client's standard PG ops procedures handle them — same as for any other PG-backed application in their stack.

## What changes architecturally

- All persistence is via DATABASE_URL connections. No more `.axon/graph_v2/*.db` filesystem state.
- Live/dev separation = 2 different DATABASE_URLs (different ports OR different databases on same instance, client choice).
- Promotion live workflow simplified: no binary swap of split-brain DBs, just a process restart pointing at the canonical DATABASE_URL.
- `./scripts/axon promote-live-safe` becomes a much simpler artifact-and-config swap.
- Backup/restore = client's PG tooling. Axon ships with NO backup utilities.
- `scripts/recovery/soll_db_rebuild.sh` (DuckDB recovery) becomes obsolete and is deleted post-migration.

## What stays under Axon's responsibility

- Schema design + DDL generator (per-project schema namespace, indexes, AGE labels, pgvector indexes).
- Migrations versioning (sqlx-cli or refinery-style versioned migration files).
- SOLL bootstrap seed (initial Visions/Pillars/etc. for a fresh deployment).
- axon_init_project scaffolding (CREATE SCHEMA + tables for a new project).
- Cypher dialect translation if AGE behavior diverges from existing tool expectations.

## Customer-value framing

Enterprise customers commonly already have PG ops expertise. They don't want yet another DB engine to operate. They DO want a structural-intelligence runtime that fits into their existing data ops surface. PG-as-backend turns Axon from 'a new DB to operate' into 'an analytical service over PG you already operate'. This dramatically lowers the adoption barrier and aligns with their compliance/audit/backup workflows.

## Operational implications

- DEC-AXO-060 4-verb canonical (start/stop/status/qualify) preserved — it's about Axon process lifecycle, which doesn't change.
- DEC-AXO-067 dual-instance discipline preserved — still 2 instances (live, dev), now distinguished by DATABASE_URL.
- PIL-AXO-001 'Shared Runtime Truth' invariant strengthened: instead of Axon-curated truth, truth is Axon's structured projection over client-owned data.

Operator (Didier) 2026-05-07 ~07:00 local: 'nous ne prenons plus de responsabilite au niveau du stockage des donnees, c'est au client d'assumer son stockage'. Formalized as a Concept by Claude Opus 4.7 [1m] 2026-05-07 ~07:35 local.

[SUPERSEDED 2026-05-08 by multi-project-tables design (option chosen by operator in 2026-05-08 architecture review).]

The per-project schema namespace introduced needless complexity: every IST writer + reader needed a `<schema>.X` qualifier resolved from project_code, threading project_code through ~30 callsites in graph_ingestion.rs and every tools_*.rs SQL surface. Cross-project queries (`retrieve_context project="*"`) became `UNION ALL` over N schemas instead of `WHERE project_code IN (...)`. HNSW indexing fragmented per schema. Operational backup / RBAC benefits did not materialise at Axon's mono-tenant deployment shape.

## New design (post-supersedure)

All IST tables live in `public` with a `project_code TEXT NOT NULL` column on every row, mirroring the DuckDB layout exactly. `generate_project_schema(project_code)` is preserved for API stability (still validates the project_code shape) but emits zero DDL — every project shares the same tables. Cross-project queries become trivial `WHERE project_code IN (...)` filters. A single global pgvector HNSW index covers all projects (filter via WHERE clause + iterative scan).

## Why this works at scale

- 10 projects × 70K files = 700K rows: PG handles this easily with composite indexes leading on `project_code`.
- DROP PROJECT becomes `DELETE WHERE project_code = $code` (idempotent, atomic in a transaction).
- DDL evolution: `ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN` applies once, not per-project.
- HNSW: pgvector iterative_scan with `WHERE project_code = $p` is efficient.

## What remains valid

Schema-name validation (`schema_name_for`) is preserved and used for `axon_runtime` schema setup + AGE label naming. The injection-rejection guard still applies even though no DDL is emitted.
